EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms producing heavy rainfall, severe winds, hail and tornadoes moved across Southeastern Kansas and into the Missouri Ozarks from May 20th through May 22th as upper level disturbances interacted with a slow moving but powerful storm system over the Central Plains. Tornadoes occurred with each round, with the strongest and most numerous tornadoes occurring during the late afternoon and evening of the 22nd. The cumulative rainfall caused areas of flash flooding with some flooding lingering across the region. Some locations in southeastern Kansas and southwestern Missouri reported in excess of 8 inches of rainfall over the 72 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted. Report received via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light rain changed over to light snow by the mid afternoon on Friday January 11. After several hours of not accumulating due to warm surface temperatures snow began to pile up shortly after sunset. While the atmosphere was certainly cold enough to produce plenty of snow it was warm enough to keep the snow to liquid ratio rather low. So, while more than 1 of liquid fell in many areas, this translated to roughly 5 to 8 inches of snow. Some of that liquid fell as rain, but the low snow to liquid ratio caused it to be a rather densely packed snow which clung to trees across the area, forming a picturesque winter wonderland. Snow continued to fall through the day on January 12, but most of the snowfall occurred through the night between January 11 and 12. Some totals in the region exceeded 12 inches, but most areas ended up with between 5 and 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 8 and 10 inches of snow fell across Atchison County, with most of it falling over the course of the first 12 hours. Light snow continued into the next day (January 12), but was fairly light, and only accounted for 1 to 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 19, 2019 a snow plow driver clearing roads from a marginal snow event was killed when his snow plow left the road and overturned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow plow driver for the Kansas Department of Transportation was killed Saturday morning after his vehicle crashed during snow removal operations.|||From KWCH12: The Kansas Highway Patrol says 25-year-old Stephen Windler, of Paola, was traveling south on U.S. 69 south of 207th Street in Johnson County when he turned his plow to the right. He crashed into the grass and fell out of the vehicle.||EMS pronounced Windler dead on the scene. No other vehicles were involved in the crash.||Link to the story: https://www.kwch.com/content/news/Snow-Plow-driver-dies-after-fatal-accident--504597511.html .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front undercut much warmer air to produce widespread freezing drizzle across Central Kansas on the 22nd. Although Winter Storm Warning criteria weren't met, travel became quite dangerous as numerous accidents occurred; many on I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads and highways were very slick across the county. Some accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-starved, weak low pressure system dropped 1 to 4 inches of snow on this Tuesday. This event really evolved as two separate areas of snow. During the mid to late morning hours, a very narrow southwest-northeast oriented band of snow developed from central Kansas, across north central Kansas, into southeast Nebraska. This band moved east and gradually became wider and better organized. The western edge of this band was most persistent over eastern Jewell and Mitchell counties. The snow ended over these counties by 7 PM CST. While this was transpiring, a separate narrow band had developed from northwest Kansas into south central Nebraska. This band moved east as well and entered Phillips and Rooks counties between 5 and 6 PM CST. This band crossed the rest of north central Kansas and exited to the east by 1 AM CST. This band was only two counties wide, but at times, there was an embedded band of heavier snow that was much more narrow, less than one county wide. Most locations only measured 1 inch from this event. The highest totals were where the snow was most persistent, over Jewell county, where 3.0 inches was measured at Lovewell Dam, and 4.1 inches at Webber. Throughout this event, the character of all the radar returns, from both areas of snow, was so poorly-organized that the snow bands exhibited a skeletal structure.||At the surface, a weak low pressure system emerged from Colorado and moved east through Kansas. This low never had a closed isobar. In the upper-levels a positively-tilted trough was over Colorado and New Mexico at daybreak. This trough advanced into Nebraska, Kansas and extreme western Texas by sunset. This was the primary trough over the CONUS, with a ridge over the eastern Pacific and a ridge along the East Coast.

Several inches of light to moderate snow fell across part of the area on this Friday and Saturday. During the daylight hours Friday, a large shield of rain and snow formed across southern Kansas, and it gradually lifted into northern Kansas. Around midday, light rain and snow began falling across Rooks, Osborne, and Mitchell counties. Any rain that was falling changed to snow and, by late afternoon, the precipitation shield had lifted further north, with snow falling across Phillips, Smith, and Jewell counties as well. By 6 PM CST, a single, long, fairly narrow deformation snow band had evolved and extended from near Garden City across north central Kansas to Nebraska City, Nebraska. This band remained nearly stationary until 1 AM CST Saturday. The band then began slowly drifting east while gradually diminishing in intensity. The snow ended from west to east, across north central Kansas, between 6 AM and 2 PM CST Saturday. All six counties were covered by a minimum of 4 to 6 inches of fresh snow. The highest amounts included 9.0 at Hunter, 8.5 inches at Plainville, 8.0 at Beloit and Randall, 6.5 at Natoma, and 6.0 at Osborne, Jewell, and Smith Center. Surface winds were fairly light with this event, averaging 5 to 10 MPH Friday afternoon and night. During the daytime Saturday, winds increased to 10 to 20 MPH, which is tame for most snowstorms here. So, problems with drifting were minimal. Because this occurred primarily on a weekend, the impacts were pretty low. ||This snowstorm occurred in the absence of any surface features. The closest surface system was a weak 1009 mb low moving east through Oklahoma. The upper-levels exhibited an omega shaped pattern with a high-amplitude ridge extending from Arizona to Idaho. A downstream trough was along the Colorado-Kansas border Friday evening, and this trough closed off into a 500 mb low along the Oklahoma-Kansas border by dawn Saturday. 300 mb analyses indicated strong divergence over the area while the band was strongest, with a coupled jet streak configuration. This band developed within  the area of deformation, in the 700-500 mb trough. Despite the appearance on radar, though, mesoanalyses did not indicate frontogenesis was occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranging from 6 to 9 inches was reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very shallow Arctic cold front pushed through Kansas on February 5th. By very early February 6th warm air and moisture just above the surface started surging north. This set the stage for widespread freezing drizzle, freezing rain along with some sleet. Warm air above the Arctic airmass at the surface also provided a setup for scattered thunderstorms, with many of them producing sleet and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of sleet and snow fell across portions of the county.   A trained spotter in Lyons, Kansas reported 0.5 inches of snow.  A CO-OP observer in Sterling, Kansas reported a tenth of an inch of sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm near Norton, KS produced wind gusts up to around 90 mph in addition to causing some tree damage in town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Norton AWOS recorded a wind gust to 89 mph. Norton County Dispatch reported numerous trees down across the town of Norton. Pictures from the local park showed some minor damage due to falling trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several bands of snow moved southeast across Northwest Kansas during the afternoon and through the night before ending.  Snowfall amounts increased to the southeast, ranging from six to twelve inches, with the highest amount reported south of St. Peter.  Due to the high snowfall rates, some locations reported poor visibility in the falling snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts ranged from two to six inches, with several reports of six to six and a half inches in the southeast part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant storm moved across Cheyenne County Colorado and Wallace County Kansas on the evening of June 21, 2019. This storm produced powerful wind gusts and hail ranging from quarter size to tennis ball size. Sporadic wind damage occurred with this storm along with damage associated with wind-driven hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm survey found additional significant wind damage in Wallace County. At this particular farmstead, numerous tree branches were broken, damage to several large metal grain bins, complete collapse of a farm building, and loss of metal roofing material of another building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of freezing drizzle occurred on this Monday. An Arctic air mass was in place with bitterly cold high pressure over the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle spreading across the area resulted in slick conditions. A woman going out to get her mail at her home, approximately 5 miles east-southeast of Esbon, slipped on ice, fell and later died. The exact time of her fall is unknown.

During the event, most of the wind gusts were just below criteria, however wind gusts increased to 35-40 MPH during the last couple hours of the event.  Up to three inches of snow was reported across Northwest Kansas during this event, with the highest amount of around five inches reported in Menlo.  By the end of the event, I-70 was closed west from Colby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to nearly 60 mph occurred early on this Monday morning. These winds occurred due to the relatively deep mixing associated with sensible heat fluxes in the presence of cold air advection. During the day Sunday, an Alberta Clipper dove south-southeast into the Dakota's. This low crossed Iowa Sunday night, and as that occurred, a pair of cold fronts swept south through north central Kansas. The polar front crossed the state between 9 PM and midnight CST Sunday, while the Arctic front quickly followed around 6 AM CST. Wind gusts between 50 and 57 mph were widespread across north central Kansas. The highest wind gust measured was 58 MPH at a rural location north of Bellaire. No significant impacts were reported from the high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 MPH gust was measured by a mesonet located 8 miles north of Bellaire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture-starved, weak low pressure system dropped 1 to 4 inches of snow on this Tuesday. This event really evolved as two separate areas of snow. During the mid to late morning hours, a very narrow southwest-northeast oriented band of snow developed from central Kansas, across north central Kansas, into southeast Nebraska. This band moved east and gradually became wider and better organized. The western edge of this band was most persistent over eastern Jewell and Mitchell counties. The snow ended over these counties by 7 PM CST. While this was transpiring, a separate narrow band had developed from northwest Kansas into south central Nebraska. This band moved east as well and entered Phillips and Rooks counties between 5 and 6 PM CST. This band crossed the rest of north central Kansas and exited to the east by 1 AM CST. This band was only two counties wide, but at times, there was an embedded band of heavier snow that was much more narrow, less than one county wide. Most locations only measured 1 inch from this event. The highest totals were where the snow was most persistent, over Jewell county, where 3.0 inches was measured at Lovewell Dam, and 4.1 inches at Webber. Throughout this event, the character of all the radar returns, from both areas of snow, was so poorly-organized that the snow bands exhibited a skeletal structure.||At the surface, a weak low pressure system emerged from Colorado and moved east through Kansas. This low never had a closed isobar. In the upper-levels a positively-tilted trough was over Colorado and New Mexico at daybreak. This trough advanced into Nebraska, Kansas and extreme western Texas by sunset. This was the primary trough over the CONUS, with a ridge over the eastern Pacific and a ridge along the East Coast.

Monetary damages are unknown despite the entry indicating 0 dollars of damages.

Strong winds and moderate to heavy snow caused blizzard conditions to occur for counties along and south of Highway 24, with whiteout conditions occurring at times.  Snowfall amounts of up to 10 inches were reported, the highest report came from southern Wallace County.  Generally snowfall amounts of six inches or higher occurred south of Highway 24.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported across the county.  Snowfall amounts ranged from four to almost six inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation supercell t-storm developed during the afternoon of May 28th across Lyon County Kansas and tracked northeast through the evening producing several tornadoes.  The strongest tornado was an EF4 violent tornado that tracked just south of Lawrence Kansas northeast into Leavenworth county Kansas.  No fatalities occurred due to the tornadoes with around 17 injuries reported with most of those associated with vehicles that were chasing the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overnight thunderstorm complex caused a brief tornado that impacted a church in rural Miami County, just south of Paola, KS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During an overnight complex of thunderstorms a QLCS tornado formed just south of Paola. The tornado impacted a church and did room damage. Several grave markers were also damaged in a nearby cemetery, and the flag pole in front of the church was bent completely over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight surface pressure gradient behind a strong low pressure system helped produce strong winds during the morning.
A tight surface pressure gradient behind a strong low pressure system helped produce strong winds during the morning.

A record or near record breaking low pressure system moved from Colorado into Nebraska in association with a very strong upper level low.  The surface pressure gradient and mixing of the boundary layer allowed for very high winds to develop.  The lowest surface pressure recorded was in Stanton county at 106 PM on the 13th when the pressure fell to 971.87 mb.  At the Dodge City airport the pressure fell to 974.6 mb with the previous record established in February of 1960 when a 974.9 mb pressure was observed.  Lower values were noted in the 1800's at Dodge City, but those numbers are suspect.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed at 5 WNW Elkhart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 2nd several east-west bands of snow developed over Northwest Kansas.  The southern band of snow, running roughly along Highway 40, produced six to seven inches of snow across parts of Wallace, Wichita, Logan, and Gove counties.  Snowfall rates were between one and two inches per hour under this band.||After the snow ended on the 3rd, the sky cleared allowing temperatures to plummet the following night.  Wind chills of 25 to almost 30 degrees below zero were reported on the morning of the 4th across most of Northwest Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seven inches of snow was reported over the south half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several months of heavy snow across the Northern Plains set the stage for a significant and catastrophic flooding event when temperatures warmed and heavy rain fell across Nebraska and the Dakotas. While the more significant damage occurred across eastern Nebraska several counties along the Missouri River in parts of northeast Kansas saw some high impacts as well. Numerous locations along the Missouri River received major flooding, in which the impacts were aggravated by by several levy failures. These failures resulted in numerous roads across the area being completely washed away and the inundation of several small towns and residential areas. While the flash flooding threats associated with the levy failures were fairly transient, the Missouri River remained in either minor or moderate flood through the rest of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Once the Missouri River started flooding on March 9, it remained in flood through the remainder of the month. The gauge in St. Joseph, Missouri reached 32.12 feet on March 22 (preliminarily a new record). The flooding was caused by several rounds of heavy snow across the Northern Plains through the winter months, followed by a rapid warm-up and several days of heavy rain. The Missouri River Basin across the Northern Plains flooded with catastrophic consequences as numerous dams and levees failed. As the water flowed into the Missouri River upstream of Buchanan County, the river swelled and ultimately overwhelmed the levees along the river. Local fields were also submerged, which delayed the growing season in the region, but several roads were washed away across Atchison County, and I-29 from St. Joseph northward to the Iowa/Missouri border was closed until mid May. Note: The dollar valued assigned to this event is likely inaccurate as a full tally of the extent of the physical damages is unknown. The Missouri River remained in flood through the rest of the month, into April.

Some resources were secured or moved off the base at Fort Leavenworth since some of the base was covered with water. The river remained in flood into the middle part of May. Note: The dollar value assigned to the damages is likely not accurate, as the full extent of the damage is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm system moved across Northwest Kansas during the afternoon from west to east.  Not long after the snow starting the high winds developed, causing the visibility to be near zero at times. Two waves of snow moved through, the first lasted into the early evening. The second wave mostly remained in East Central Colorado, but did extend into Northwest Kansas during the early evening as it weakened.  After the snow had ended the high winds continued into the following day. The peak wind gust was 85 MPH measured at Weskan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility was reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds and near blizzard conditions occurred on this Wednesday night and Thursday. On Monday and Tuesday (March 11th and 12th), a low pressure system progressed through Arizona and New Mexico. Tuesday night, this low began rapidly deepening over Colorado. In just 15 h, this low deepened 24 mb from 994 mb to 970 mb. This low broke the all-time sea-level pressure record for the state of Colorado, with 970.4 mb at Lamar. The previous record was 975.0 mb on March 13, 1973. ||Periods of rain occurred with this system from late Tuesday night into Wednesday ahead of the occluded front. The rain temporarily ended by 10 AM CST Wednesday, as the front moved north into Nebraska. The next batch of rain developed and blossomed over western Kansas Wednesday afternoon, due to destabilization associated with daytime heating under the upper low. This resulted in another band of rain moving across north central Kansas late Wednesday afternoon and evening. As the upper-level low moved from northwest Kansas and into Nebraska Wednesday night, a small deformation zone of rain evolved over Nebraska, with its southern edge extending south into north central Kansas. As cold air advection cooled the lower troposphere, the rain changed to snow after midnight. Snow fell primarily across Phillips, Smith, and Jewell counties from 1 AM CST to 9 AM CST Thursday. This snow fell during windy conditions. By sunrise Thursday, the surface low had filled, but was still 984 mb over western Iowa, with a 22 mb pressure gradient across Kansas, from northeast to southwest. ||Windy conditions occurred from Wednesday evening into Thursday, but there were two periods in which high winds occurred. The first was immediately after the passage of the 980 mb low Wednesday evening. A few gusts were measured as high as 58 mph over Rooks county. One of them was from official measuring equipment at the Rooks County airport. The second period was Thursday around midday, after the snow ended. Again, only one of the gusts occurred with official measuring equipment. That gust of 58 mph occurred at the Rooks County airport. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon the warm side of a winter storm system moved across Northwest Kansas.  Hail up to nickel size was reported at Angelus, with slightly smaller hail reported near Grinnell.  Pea size hail was reported in Greeley, Logan, Thomas, Graham and Norton counties. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was nickel size, with some stones a little larger in size.  The roads were completely covered with hail.

Two to four inches of snow fell across north central Kansas on this Saturday night and Sunday. A large shield of light snow moved east out of Colorado into Kansas during the early to mid-evening hours. Between 10 PM and 12 AM CST, this area of snow overspread north central Kansas. Snow continued steadily falling through 4 AM CST. Between 4 and 6 AM CST, the snow ended from west to east. Snowfall amounts averaged 2 to 4 inches. ||A bitterly cold Arctic air mass encompassed the central United States. High pressure over Montana was 1048 mb. The flow aloft did not depict how cold temperatures were at the surface. The flow was zonal, but a subtle shortwave trough extended from Kansas south to northern Texas. A very cold 510 dm low was over northern Ontario. The shield of light snow was produced from a combination of mid-level frontogenesis and quasi-geostrophic forcing associated with a a coupled jet streak configuration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches across the county.

The highest wind gust reported was estimated at 70 MPH in Bogue, which blew a 10 ft. flatbed trailer 50 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated the wind gusts to be 50-60 MPH.

A large shield of light snow moved east out of Colorado into Kansas during the early to mid-evening hours. Between 10 PM and 12 AM CST, this area of snow overspread north central Kansas. Snow continued steadily falling through 4 AM CST. Between 4 and 6 AM CST, the snow ended from west to east. Snowfall amounts averaged 2 to 4 inches. ||A bitterly cold Arctic air mass encompassed the central United States. High pressure over Montana was 1048 mb. The flow aloft did not depict how cold temperatures were at the surface. The flow was zonal, but a subtle shortwave trough extended from Kansas south to northern Texas. A very cold 510 dm low was over northern Ontario. The shield of light snow was produced from a combination of mid-level frontogenesis and quasi-geostrophic forcing associated with a a coupled jet streak configuration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet snow fell over much of northeast Kansas beginning around 6pm Friday January 11th and ending by early morning on Saturday January 12th.  Most spots in the area received 3-6 inches of snow but a few areas saw more widespread 5 to 6 inch amounts including parts of Osage, Franklin, Anderson into Douglas Counties.  The heavy moisture content of the snow weighed down tree limbs and did cause tree limb damage across parts of Osage, Franklin, Anderson and Douglas Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from COOP, EM and NWS employees showed that Douglas County received 5-6 inches of snow.  Reports from NWS employee also indicated minor tree limb damage due to the weight of the snow on trees in the city of Lawrence.  There was little wind associated with the snowfall so high water content or heavy weight of snow accumulated on tree limbs and did cause some to break.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail occurred in extreme northeast Smith county on this Thursday morning. Between 5 and 6 AM CST, isolated thunderstorms developed just north of the state line in extreme southern Nebraska. Thunderstorms continued to increase through the morning hours, and they trained eastward just north of the Kansas state line. The strongest and most dominant storm was the first storm that developed. That storm formed over Furnas county Nebraska, and as it moved east, a small part of this storm extended into extreme northern Smith county between 745 and 830 AM CST. Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported at 820 AM over the northeast corner of the county.||During the nighttime hours, a strong cold front had been sagging south through Nebraska. By dawn, it had arrived across northern Kansas, but its forward progress had slowed to the point that it had temporarily become stationary. While this occurred, weak low pressure moved out of southeast Colorado into southwest Kansas. This increased the lift atop the front which resulted in the elevated storms atop the cooler air mass. The morning radiosonde launch at North Platte, NE indicated southeast winds at 30 kt at 850 mb, just above the frontal surface. The upper-level flow was zonal across the majority of the U.S., but observed flow in the mid-levels showed that there was a subtle shortwave trough at 700 mb. MUCAPE was just over 1000 J/kg with very steep mid-level lapse rates of 8 C/km. Deep layer shear from the lifting condensation level was between 35 and 40 kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred near the town of Codell on this Saturday afternoon. A strong 990 mb low pressure system moved from southwest to northeast across Kansas during the morning, and across Missouri into Illinois during the afternoon. Windy conditions occurred due to the strong pressure gradient west of this low. The pressure at nearby Russell rapidly increased 11 mb during the 6 hr time period from 9 AM to 3 PM CST. Northwest winds were sustained at 30 to 35 mph, with most gusts between 40 and 50 mph. However, an unofficial weather station in Codell gusted to 58 mph around midday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 MPH wind gust was measured by a mesonet located 8 miles north-northeast of Codell.

The highest amounts greater than 3 inches were listed individually.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain and freezing drizzle fell across much of north central and northeast Kansas during the day on January 22nd.  The freezing rain changed to snow and accumulated from 1 to 6 inches across the NWS Topeka service area.  Only a small area received up to 6 inches while most spots received 1-3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official NWS snow observer reported 6.5 inches measured in Concordia.  Other reports from various sources indicated widespread 4 to 6 inch amounts in addition to around one tenth of an inch of ice accumulation from freezing rain earlier in the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe t-storms moved across north central Kansas and did produce one small weak tornado in Washington County around 920 pm CDT Sunday May 26th. An outbuilding was destroyed and windows were broken on another school building in Linn, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos submitted show degree of damage to outbuilding and windows at a school suggest higher end EF0 type damage along a short path in Linn, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell t-storms developed quickly along an arc from north central east southeast through northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and early evening on Tuesday May 21st. The supercells occurred in an environment conducive to rapid low level rotation and tornado formation.  At least 6 tornadoes were documented between 4pm and 9 pm during that evening.  Some were longer track tornadoes with the strongest being rated an EF3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to EM who was following the tornado, it stayed in rural areas of Dickinson county damaging trees and power lines.  The damage track was sporadic indicating that the circulation may have lifted briefly along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon the warm side of a winter storm system moved across Northwest Kansas.  Hail up to nickel size was reported at Angelus, with slightly smaller hail reported near Grinnell.  Pea size hail was reported in Greeley, Logan, Thomas, Graham and Norton counties.  During the evening the rain changed to snow as the cold side of the winter storm system moved across Northwest Kansas.  The following morning blizzard conditions were reported at Goodland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than two inches of snow was reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell t-storms developed quickly along an arc from north central east southeast through northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and early evening on Tuesday May 21st. The supercells occurred in an environment conducive to rapid low level rotation and tornado formation.  At least 6 tornadoes were documented between 4pm and 9 pm during that evening.  Some were longer track tornadoes with the strongest being rated an EF3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado formed near Mayetta and moved through the western side of town with EF1 damage noted to outbuildings and trees.  The damage track was intermittent northeast of this point but there were several structures damaged including a house near the end of the track that suggested low end EF2 damage.  No injuries were reported.

Very moist and unstable conditions developed over Southeast Kansas ahead of an intense low pressure system over the Southern Plains. Widespread severe storms developed over Oklahoma and eastern Kansas during the early morning hours and tracked eastward into the eastern Kansas counties of the Springfield County Warning Area during the afternoon. Very heavy rainfall and straight line wind damage occurred with this system as storms tracked repeatedly over the region. The resulting rainfall led to flash flooding on head water streams and low water crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersections of South Broadway Street and Ford Avenue, and 4th Street near Highway 69 were reported as water covered and impassable to traffic. Heavy rainfall was continuing to fall at the time of the report. Several pictures from social media showed water ponding in yards and on streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Part of northeast Kansas were buffeted by strong northwest winds that gusted from 50 to 60 mph during the morning of Monday January 28th.  A semi was overturned on Interstate 70 between Topeka and Lawrence due to the high winds on Monday morning.  ASOS winds also gusted to between 55 and 60 mph at Topeka and Lawrence Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow and northwest winds increased to between 40 and 50 mph during the day on Saturday February 23rd.  Blizzard conditions developed across much of central and north central Kansas during the day into the early evening on February 23.  Snow measurements were quite variable as expected with such conditions.  Most areas that had verifiable blizzard conditions received 3 to 9 inches of snow.  Blizzard conditions occurred approximately from 10 am CST through around 6 pm CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KDOT and county EM reported zero visibility at times with prevailing visibility of less than one quarter mile for much of late morning through mid afternoon.  Winds of 35-50 mph combined with heavy falling and blowing snow to create blizzard conditions. Snowfall estimates ranged from 6 to 8 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly low-impact snow event occurred within this six-county North Central Kansas area, getting underway on the late afternoon and evening of Tuesday the 19th and lingering into the morning of Wednesday the 20th before ending. Most places only received between 1.5 and 3, with a few of the slightly higher exceptions noted within eastern portions of Jewell and Mitchell counties, including CoCoRaHS reports of 5.1 near Scottsville and 4 near Webber, and an NWS cooperative observer measurement of 4 at Beloit. Greatly contributing to the lower-impact nature of this event were light easterly winds, only averaging 5-15 MPH and keeping blowing/drifting to a minimum. Timing-wise, the vast majority of accumulation occurred between 5 p.m. on the 19th and 5 a.m. on the 20th, with only pockets of light snow/flurries lingering through around 7 a.m. before ending. In the mid-upper levels, this event was driven by a potent shortwave trough swinging north-northeast through the Central Plains, along the front-side of a larger-scale, highly-amplified trough anchored over the Rockies. At the surface, temperatures averaged 20-25 F during most of the snow, with the aforementioned, relatively-light easterly winds fostered by return-flow around the western fringes of an expansive ridge centered over the northeastern United States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3.0 inches occurred in Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged freezing drizzle and freezing rain event impacted all of eastern Kansas and western and central Missouri from February 5 until February 7. While the bulk of the precipitation fell overnight February 6, into the morning of February 7 the higher impacts appeared to come on February 5, when freezing drizzle caused roads to become slick. In some areas the roads were completely impassible, and numerous injury vehicle accidents occurred. One fatality was caused by the icy roads on February 5 in Miami County. ||Without any notable break in the freezing precipitation the ice continued to form on trees and power lines through Wednesday February 6, resulting in most locations seeing around 1/8 to 1/4 of ice through Wednesday afternoon. On Wednesday night heavy rain and sleet moved into the area, and added to the already robust ice amounts. With some modest instability in the atmosphere many of these storms produced lightning, a rare occurrence! One storm did produce some quarter-sized hail near Clinton, MO. By the end of the event almost all locations within the Ice Storm Warning (which was issued Wednesday afternoon - 2/6) received around 1/4 to 1/2, with some of the higher totals in Johnson County, MO approaching .60.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per the St. Joseph Post: One person died in an accident just before 11 a.m. Tuesday in Miami County.||The Kansas Highway Patrol reported a 2002 Chevy Silverado driven by Dennis J. Gentry, 64, Mound City, was southbound on U.S. 169 North of 355th Street.||The vehicle was traveling too fast for road conditions. The driver lost control of the pickup as it crossed the bridge. The pickup traveled off the roadway on the left, struck the bridge. reentered the roadway, slid sideways  and overturned.||Gentry was pronounced dead at the scene and transported to Frontier Forensics. He was not wearing a seat belt, according to the KHP.||Link to the story: http://www.stjosephpost.com/2019/02/05/kansas-man-dies-after-pickup-traveling-too-fast-hits-bridge-overturns/ .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged freezing drizzle and freezing rain event impacted all of eastern Kansas and western and central Missouri from February 5 until February 7. While the bulk of the precipitation fell overnight February 6, into the morning of February 7 the higher impacts appeared to come on February 5, when freezing drizzle caused roads to become slick. In some areas the roads were completely impassible, and numerous injury vehicle accidents occurred. One fatality was caused by the icy roads on February 5 in Miami County. ||Without any notable break in the freezing precipitation the ice continued to form on trees and power lines through Wednesday February 6, resulting in most locations seeing around 1/8 to 1/4 of ice through Wednesday afternoon. On Wednesday night heavy rain and sleet moved into the area, and added to the already robust ice amounts. With some modest instability in the atmosphere many of these storms produced lightning, a rare occurrence! One storm did produce some quarter-sized hail near Clinton, MO. By the end of the event almost all locations within the Ice Storm Warning (which was issued Wednesday afternoon - 2/6) received around 1/4 to 1/2, with some of the higher totals in Johnson County, MO approaching .60.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While light freezing drizzle occurred off and on February 5, the bulk of the freezing rain fell during the overnight period on February 6 into February 7. Over the course of the event Atchison County received approximately a quarter inch of ice accumulation. Numerous vehicle accidents occurred area-wide and minor tree damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface cyclone that erupted over New Mexico on the 22nd likewise surged northeast across Kansas on the 23rd. reaching the Iowa/Missouri border early that evening. The potent tandem produced a blizzard across most of the northern half of Kansas where sustained winds from 30 to 45 mph with 50 to 55 mph gusts combined with heavy snow to cause near zero visibilities across nearly all of Central Kansas. Snowfalls from 4 to 7 inches were common and with such strong winds, many roads and highways were closed; among them was I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 210 PM, a trained spotter 6 miles south/southwest of Waldo reported heavy snow that combined with 30 to 40 mph winds to cause severe visibility restrictions that were less than 100 yards. At 1235 PM, this same trained spotter had estimated 4 to 5 inches of heavy snow that, along with 25 to 30 mph winds, reduced visibilities to 1/4 mile. At 145 PM, a storm chaser estimated 2 to 3 inches of heavy snow in Russell. At 336 PM, the law enforcement in Russell reported that heavy snow combined with blowing snow to reduce visibilities to less than 1/8 of a mile. At this same time, they had estimated 7-inch accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface cyclone that erupted over New Mexico on the 22nd likewise surged northeast across Kansas on the 23rd. reaching the Iowa/Missouri border early that evening. The potent tandem produced a blizzard across most of the northern half of Kansas where sustained winds from 30 to 45 mph with 50 to 55 mph gusts combined with heavy snow to cause near zero visibilities across nearly all of Central Kansas. Snowfalls from 4 to 7 inches were common and with such strong winds, many roads and highways were closed; among them was I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 233 PM, a trained spotter 2 miles north of Ash Grove reported 30 to 40 mph winds and heavy snow caused very poor vsibilities. At this time, he had estimated 5-inch accumulations. At 336 PM, a trained spotter in Lincoln reported heavy snow and blowing snow that caused severe visibility restrictions around 1/8 mile. The spotter had estimated 4-inch accumulations. The blizzard produced 6 to 7 inch accumulations in most of Lincoln County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged freezing drizzle and freezing rain event impacted all of eastern Kansas and western and central Missouri from February 5 until February 7. While the bulk of the precipitation fell overnight February 6, into the morning of February 7 the higher impacts appeared to come on February 5, when freezing drizzle caused roads to become slick. In some areas the roads were completely impassible, and numerous injury vehicle accidents occurred. One fatality was caused by the icy roads on February 5 in Miami County. ||Without any notable break in the freezing precipitation the ice continued to form on trees and power lines through Wednesday February 6, resulting in most locations seeing around 1/8 to 1/4 of ice through Wednesday afternoon. On Wednesday night heavy rain and sleet moved into the area, and added to the already robust ice amounts. With some modest instability in the atmosphere many of these storms produced lightning, a rare occurrence! One storm did produce some quarter-sized hail near Clinton, MO. By the end of the event almost all locations within the Ice Storm Warning (which was issued Wednesday afternoon - 2/6) received around 1/4 to 1/2, with some of the higher totals in Johnson County, MO approaching .60.

Over the course of the event Linn County received approximately a quarter inch of ice accumulation. Numerous vehicle accidents occurred area-wide and minor tree damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very shallow Arctic cold front pushed through Kansas on February 5th. By very early February 6th warm air and moisture just above the surface started surging north. This set the stage for widespread freezing drizzle, freezing rain along with some sleet. Warm air above the Arctic airmass at the surface also provided a setup for scattered thunderstorms, with many of them producing sleet and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of sleet and freezing rain fell across the county.  There was even some thunder reported with the sleet.   A CO-OP observer in Moran, Kansas reported 0.40 inches of sleet accumulation and a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation from freezing rain.  The public also reported an inch of sleet accumulation in Iola, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning snow began moving up from the south into Northwest Kansas.  The northward bound bands of snow eventually met the snow moving east out of Colorado.   As the day progressed multiple bands of snow formed over Northwest Kansas.  The snow ended from west to east during the afternoon.  Strong winds and moderate to heavy snow caused blizzard conditions to occur for counties along and south of Highway 24, with whiteout conditions occurring at times.  Snowfall amounts of up to 10 inches were reported, the highest report came from southern Wallace County.  Generally snowfall amounts of six inches or higher occurred south of Highway 24.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of a quarter mile or less was reported in the county.  Snowfall amounts ranged from two to six inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For most of this six-county North Central Kansas area, the quick-hitting-but-significant winter storm and blizzard that struck on this Saturday was likely the overall most-impactful storm of the 2018-19 winter season. This storm resulted in several hours of white-out conditions, promoting treacherous to near-impossible travel. As for snow amounts, the majority of the area received at least 6-8, with localized higher totals to around 10. Per several CoCoRaHS and NWS cooperative observers, some of the highest official totals featured: 10 near Plainville, 9 at Covert, 8.9 near Webber and 8.3 at Webster Dam. The lowest totals focused within central and especially western Phillips County, where 2-5 was common. Of course, winds were a major factor in the blizzard conditions, as sustained northerly speeds averaged 25-30 MPH with gusts commonly 35-40 MPH, frequently reducing visibility to around 1/4 mile or less.||Breaking down timing, this was roughly a nine-hour storm for the area as a whole. The first moderate-heavy snow bands blossomed between 9-11 a.m., peaked in intensity during the early-mid afternoon, and then gradually ended from west-to-east during the late afternoon and early evening, with even the far eastern counties along Highway 14 mostly snow-free by 7 p.m. In the mid-upper levels, this was a fairly classic recipe for a major winter storm/blizzard, as a powerful shortwave trough started off centered over the TX Panhandle on the morning of the 23rd, reached the KS/NE/MO border area by sunset and gradually departed across IA into WI through the night. The associated strong surface low pressure system followed a very similar track. Around the time snow broke out in North Central Kansas, the low was centered over south central KS with a central pressure around 1000 millibars. Slight intensification commenced through the afternoon and evening as it headed northeast, registering around 996 millibars over southeast IA by 9 p.m. From a forecast perspective this was a well-anticipated event, with a Winter Storm Watch first issued for all six local counties on the afternoon of the 21st, around 42-44 hours before the onset of heavy snow and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 10.0 inches occurred four miles west-northwest of Plainville and 8.3 inches fell at Webster Dam. Frequent north wind gusts of 35 to 40 mph produced quarter mile visibility or less when accompanied by the heavy snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 4.0 inches occurred in Beloit (NWS observer) and 5.1 was reported by a CoCoRaHS observer south-southwest of Scottsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy late winter and spring precipitation, mixed with a loaded snow pack caused the Missouri River to flood in March. Consistent heavy rains continued the Missouri River flooding into and through June. Some locations along the Missouri River were in major flood for several weeks as heavy rains continued. While the flooding continued through June, there was some gradual and steady improvement across the Missouri River flood plain by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy spring rains caused ongoing flooding along the Missouri River to Continue through the month. Some locations along the Missouri River experienced major flooding at times during the month. Damage estimates from roads washed out and crop damage are unknown at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation supercell t-storm developed during the afternoon of May 28th across Lyon County Kansas and tracked northeast through the evening producing several tornadoes.  The strongest tornado was an EF4 violent tornado that tracked just south of Lawrence Kansas northeast into Leavenworth county Kansas.  No fatalities occurred due to the tornadoes with around 17 injuries reported with most of those associated with vehicles that were chasing the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very early in the morning on the 22nd, severe thunderstorms hit much of Central Kansas with a trifecta of large hail, damaging winds and flash floods. Of the 3 modes, damaging winds and flash floods posed, by far, the greatest threats. The flash flooding hit Marion County especially hard where in Peabody, businesses were flooded and mandatory evacuations were enforced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding that reached 2 feet prompted authorities to close the 9th Street Bridge.

A lone strong to severe thunderstorm drifted across central and southeastern Osborne County on this early Tuesday evening, prompting reports of hail up to half dollar size a few miles south-southeast of Osborne and nickel size hail in the far southeast reaches of the county. By 7:30 p.m. CDT this storm departed the local area into central Kansas. Following a lull in vigorous convection for the next few hours, another storm intensified to radar-indicated severe thresholds over southeastern Jewell County around 10 p.m. CDT. However, this one yielded no ground-truth verification and soon weakened in northern Mitchell County, ending the threat of severe storms within North Central Kansas for the night. This rather low-key severe weather event was sparked by seasonably-limited low-level moisture and modest convergence along a passing cold front. Although early evening surface dewpoints only averaged in the mid-upper 50s in the Osborne County area, isolated strong to severe convection was supported by mixed-layer CAPE of at least 1000 J/kg and deep-layer wind shear around 40 knots. In the mid-upper levels, the surface front was tied to a large-scale trough sinking south-southeast into the Central Plains and Midwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the morning on June 23rd, incredibly powerful severe thunderstorms tore through South-Central and Southeast Kansas with destructive winds that reached a hideous 111 mph/97 knots. Prolific damaging wind-producers, the severe thunderstorms caused tremendous damage in Derby, a Southeast Wichita suburb, with sustained speeds measured at 76 mph/66 knots by a Mesonet Wunderground Ambient Weather Station 1 mile northwest of town and where the 111-knot/97-mph gust was also measured just before daybreak. The severe thunderstorms also spawned 2 tornadoes in Harper County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a delayed report from local law enforcement.  No damage was noted with this tornado.

Although thunderstorms steered clear of the vast majority of this six-county North Central Kansas area on Friday evening the 14th into early Saturday morning the 15th, a noticeable exception occurred in northern Jewell County. There, a large severe storm gradually slipped south of the state line out of Nuckolls County Nebraska between 11 p.m. and 1 a.m. CDT, with a CoCoRaHS observer near Webber reporting quarter size hail along with 2.33 of overnight rain. Mainly due to heavier rain that drenched much of Nuckolls County, minor flooding occurred along the Republican River in far northeastern Jewell County, with the automated gauge south of Hardy Nebraska cresting at 11.66 ft. during the very early morning hours of Sunday the 16th (flood stage 11.0 ft.). The severe storm that eventually impacted northern Jewell County initiated slightly north of the state line between 9:30-10:30 p.m. CDT along a subtle, west-east surface front. In the mid-upper levels, a broad quasi-zonal flow regime was present, featuring passing low-amplitude disturbances. The mesoscale environment featured a relatively potent combination of 2000-3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and around 30 knots of deep-layer wind shear. In addition, convection was aided by warm air/moisture advection along the northwestern fringes of a nocturnal low level jet primarily aligned from southern Kansas into Missouri.

Rain changed to snow and northwest winds increased to between 40 and 50 mph during the day on Saturday February 23rd.  Blizzard conditions developed across much of central and north central Kansas during the day into the early evening on February 23.  Snow measurements were quite variable as expected with such conditions.  Most areas that had verifiable blizzard conditions received 3 to 9 inches of snow.  Blizzard conditions occurred approximately from 10 am CST through around 6 pm CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather conditions varied widely across Riley county however reports from county highways and public documented blizzard conditions across the northwest half of the county during the late afternoon hours.  Snowfall estimates ranged from 3 to 5 inches across the northern half of the county.  Winds of 35 to 50 mph combined with heavy falling and blowing snow to create blizzard conditions mainly north of the Keats area for several hours late Saturday afternoon.

Snowfall estimates ranged from 2 inches across the south to 5 inches across the northwest in Marshall county.

Supercell t-storms developed quickly along an arc from north central east southeast through northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and early evening on Tuesday May 21st. The supercells occurred in an environment conducive to rapid low level rotation and tornado formation.  At least 6 tornadoes were documented between 4pm and 9 pm during that evening.  Some were longer track tornadoes with the strongest being rated an EF3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado formed around 1-2 miles northeast of Chapman and tracked into Geary County. Formed near interstate 70 northeast of Chapman based on spotter reports.  No damage reports have been received from the track in Dickinson and Geary Counties.  This is the segment that traveled across Geary county including parts of Milford State Park and ended  on Ft. Riley Army Base grounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy late winter and spring precipitation, mixed with a loaded snow pack caused the Missouri River to flood in March. Consistent heavy rains continued the Missouri River flooding into and through June. Some locations along the Missouri River were in major flood for several weeks as heavy rains continued. While the flooding continued through June, there was some gradual and steady improvement across the Missouri River flood plain by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy spring rains caused ongoing flooding along the Missouri River to Continue through the month. Some locations along the Missouri River experienced major flooding at times during the month. Damage estimates from roads washed out and crop damage are unknown at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although limited in areal coverage and yielding only a few ground-truth reports, several rounds of strong to marginally-severe thunderstorms affected this six-county North Central Kansas area during the calendar day of Saturday the 22nd. First, between midnight and 1:30 a.m. CDT, an isolated storm with radar-indicated hail potential (but no verifying reports) tracked across far southern Rooks and southwestern Osborne counties before dissipating. Then, later in the night/early morning (mainly 3-6 a.m. CDT), a complex of storms roamed through much of the area from southwest-to-northeast. While the vast majority of this activity was sub-severe, a mesonet in southern Mitchell County clocked a 61 MPH gust. Finally, following a lull in storm activity through most of the day, the left-split of a cluster of strong to severe storms that initiated over central Kansas wandered northward through eastern Mitchell and southeastern Jewell counties between 5-7 p.m. CDT, producing nickel to quarter size hail in Beloit. In the mid-upper levels, an active weather pattern for the Central Plains was firmly in place on the 22nd, featuring seasonably-strong southwesterly flow, downstream from a large-scale trough centered over the Central and Northern Rockies. At the surface, the local area resided along a north-south oriented quasi-stationary front, serving as a dividing line between a humid and very unstable airmass over much of the eastern half of Kansas and a drier airmass in the western half of the Sunflower State. As evidence of this boundary, at 4 p.m. CDT, surface dewpoints near the local area ranged from 56 F at Hill City to 70 F at Concordia.
A series of storms moved across Central Kansas dropping copious amounts of hail, some of which were the size of golf balls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed a large tree.  They also experienced dime to nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On a day with very strong wind gusts, a wildfire breaks out roughly 6 miles west of Herndon, KS in Rawlins County. The fire burns approximately 1500 acres and results in some cattle losses. In addition, 2 wooden bridges are destroyed, one for a county road and one for a railroad.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On a day with very strong wind gusts, a wildfire broke out roughly 6 miles west of Herndon, KS in Rawlins County. The fire burned approximately 1500 acres and resulted in some cattle and wildlife losses. In addition, 2 wooden bridges were destroyed, one for a county road and one for a railroad. Some uninhabited farmsteads were burned, but all inhabited structures were saved from the fire. Mutual aid from several surrounding counties and cities helped respond to the fire to keep it from having worse impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this six-county North Central Kansas area was spared the considerable wind damage that impacted several Nebraska counties within 25-50 miles north of the state line, there were still a few severe-criteria wind reports during the early morning hours of the 21st as the southern ends of two separate linear mesoscale convective systems (MCS) tracked through. The first storm complex traversed the area between midnight-3 a.m. CDT, mainly favoring locations north of the Highway 24 corridor. Upon first entering Phillips County, a mesonet near Logan clocked a 59 MPH wind gust. After this first system departed local counties to the east, there was a brief lull in convection before the second MCS arrived from the northwest, entering Phillips and Smith counties between 4:30-5:30 a.m. CDT. This system intensified a bit as it migrated east-southeast, producing a 60 MPH gust at Burr Oak (estimated) and 62 MPH near Webber (measured). The back edge of this second MCS departed the local area out of Jewell and Mitchell counties by 7:30 a.m. CDT, finally ending the stormy night. ||Looking at the meteorological setup, this was a fairly classic recipe for intense overnight convection in the Central Plains. In the mid-upper levels, seasonably-strong, west-southwesterly flow was in place, downstream from a large-scale trough centered over the Northern Rockies. At the surface, North Central Kansas resided directly along a west-east oriented front.  As a low-amplitude disturbance entered primarily southern Nebraska during the evening, this forcing combined with a very unstable airmass and increasing moisture convergence within the exit region of a 30-40 knot southerly low level jet to spark the MCS activity. By late-night standards, it was a fairly volatile mesoscale environment, featuring 2000-4000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and 30-40 knots of deep-layer wind shear. Fortunately for North Central Kansas, the most intense portion of the night's first MCS remained slightly north of the state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located 1 mile west of Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 7-9 p.m. CDT on this Tuesday evening, an intense and outflow-dominant storm cluster dropped southward out of Nebraska into primarily Jewell County of this six-county North Central Kansas area, yielding a handful of severe hail and wind reports in a brief time. A few of the more notable reports included golf ball size hail and a measured 66 MPH gust at Formoso, and half dollar size hail and estimated 65 MPH winds near Webber. To the west of the main storm, the combo of radar signatures and nearby reports from extreme southern Nebraska suggested that hail might have also slipped into far northern Smith and northeastern Phillips counties, but there were no verifying reports. Between 8-9 p.m. CDT the severe weather threat quickly waned within the local area as the most vigorous convection departed off to the east-southeast. Backing up in time, the Jewell County severe storm initiated around 4 p.m. CDT as an isolated, high-precipitation supercell roughly 40 miles north of the state line, gradually acquiring a more linear nature before entering North Central Kansas a few hours later. This was a fairly weakly-forced event in the mid-upper levels, with northern Kansas positioned along the southern periphery of broad westerly flow, and no significant shortwave troughs of note. However, the origins of this early-evening convection could be traced to low-level convergence along a west-east oriented, quasi-stationary surface front draped across south central Nebraska. The mesoscale environment became quite supportive of severe storms on either side of the state line, featuring 2000-3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.
Another round of severe weather moved across South Central Kansas producing significant winds and a couple of isolated severe hail reports.

Severe thunderstorms broke out across Barton & Lincoln Counties in the evening. Then, as evening progressed, the severe thunderstorms spread across most of the remainder of Central Kansas, then across South-Central Kansas overnight, before finally weakening as they approached Greenwood County early in the morning on the 27th. The severe thunderstorms started as hail-producers, then as they surged across Central & South-Central Kansas, became prolific damaging wind-producers with speeds that reached around 61 knots/70 mph. There were also areas of flash flooding, though not nearly as widespread as had been the case for most of the month.

A stalled frontal boundary was draped across portions of Kansas during the day on May 7th, 2019 ahead of an approaching storm system. Widespread showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall developed across the area. These storms inundated areas that were already saturated from recent rainfall resulting in widespread flooding across south central Kansas. ||Significant flash flooding occurred during the evening and overnight of May 7th, 2019 into the early morning hours of May 8th. The most significant flash flooding occurred during the early morning hours of May 8th, when areas southeast of Wichita picked up between seven and ten inches of rainfall in a 24 hour period. Numerous water rescues took place across Mulvane, Augusta, Wellington and Rose Hill. In addition, several miles of the Kansas Turnpike was closed south of Mulvane due to the Slate Creek reaching record levels. Closing this much of the Kansas Turnpike due to flooding hasn't happened in over 30 years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous county roads were reported with water over them.  The report was relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of supercell thunderstorms developed along a slow moving cold front, during the afternoon and evening of May 6th, 2019 into the early morning hours of May 7th. The supercells produced large hail and some damaging winds. As the storms moved south, they began to stall over southern Kansas and produce some flash flooding.  Significant flooding started during the late night hours of May 7th, 2019 when Chanute picked up 5 to 6 inches of rainfall in only a couple hours. This caused widespread flash flooding across the city where numerous water rescues took place.

A couple reports of low-end severe hail occurred in Phillips and Jewell counties on this Thursday. The southern part of a fairly large shield of rain moved northeast across north central Kansas from late morning through mid-afternoon. A few thunderstorms developed and one of them produced quarter size hail at Lovewell State Park early in the afternoon. Behind this area of rain, scattered, poorly-organized thunderstorms moved in from western Kansas. The strongest of these storms was an elevated supercell that crossed Phillips county from 230 to 330 PM CST. This storm produced 1 inch hail at Logan.||These storms were elevated as the area was on the cold side of the low pressure system that moved through on the 22nd. By the time these severe thunderstorms occurred, the low was exiting the Great Lakes. The cold front extended southwest, across southern Kansas, to another low pressure system over Colorado and New Mexico. High pressure was over Nebraska. The flow aloft was highly-amplified with southwest flow over the central Plains a longwave trough and upper low were over the Western U.S., and a ridge was over the East. Surface temperatures and dewpoints were in the 50s. The mid-level lapse rate was around 7.5 C/km, and MUCAPE was between 1000 and 1500 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 60 kt.

Significant flooding started during the late night hours of May 7th, 2019 when Chanute picked up 5 to 6 inches of rainfall in only a couple hours. This caused widespread flash flooding across the city where numerous water rescues took place.

A stalled frontal boundary was draped across portions of Kansas during the day on May 7th, 2019 ahead of an approaching storm system. Widespread showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall developed across the area. These storms inundated areas that were already saturated from recent rainfall resulting in widespread flooding across south central Kansas. ||Significant flash flooding occurred during the evening and overnight of May 7th, 2019 into the early morning hours of May 8th. The most significant flash flooding occurred during the early morning hours of May 8th, when areas southeast of Wichita picked up between seven and ten inches of rainfall in a 24 hour period. Numerous water rescues took place across Mulvane, Augusta, Wellington and Rose Hill. In addition, several miles of the Kansas Turnpike was closed south of Mulvane due to the Slate Creek reaching record levels. Closing this much of the Kansas Turnpike due to flooding hasn't happened in over 30 years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roadways were reported under water and impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed across portions of central Kansas, during the late afternoon and evening of May 5th, 2019.  The supercells initially produced large hail, but quickly became tornado producing storms, as they moved east southeast.  Five different tornadoes ending touching down across Barton, Rice and Reno counties, as the supercell moved to the southeast.  As the supercell moved into the Wichita metro area, the storm produced copious amounts of small hail, that covered ground to almost a foot deep, on the west side of Wichita.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were impacted by high water from Chase to Sterling in southern Rice county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of severe thunderstorms that produced a grand slam of flash floods, large hail, damaging winds & even a couple tornadoes hit nearly all of the Wichita (ICT) County Warning Area (CWA) throughout the afternoon & night on the 24th. Of the severe thunderstorm modes, floods & flash floods proved to be, by far, the greatest threat to these areas. The flash flooding continued across primarily Southeast Kansas well into the 25th and in parts of South-Central Kansas, the morning of the 26th. River flooding continued the following week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Burma Rd. was flooded in several places from the McPherson County line to Falun Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell t-storms developed quickly along an arc from north central east southeast through northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and early evening on Tuesday May 21st. The supercells occurred in an environment conducive to rapid low level rotation and tornado formation.  At least 6 tornadoes were documented between 4pm and 9 pm during that evening.  Some were longer track tornadoes with the strongest being rated an EF3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado formed around 1-2 miles northeast of Chapman and tracked into Geary County. Formed near interstate 70 northeast of Chapman based on spotter reports.  No damage reports have been received from the track in Dickinson and Geary Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms broke out across Barton & Lincoln Counties in the evening. Then, as evening progressed, the severe thunderstorms spread across most of the remainder of Central Kansas, then across South-Central Kansas overnight, before finally weakening as they approached Greenwood County early in the morning on the 27th. The severe thunderstorms started as hail-producers, then as they surged across Central & South-Central Kansas, became prolific damaging wind-producers with speeds that reached around 61 knots/70 mph. There were also areas of flash flooding, though not nearly as widespread as had been the case for most of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed was blown around 100 yards & doors were blown off a barn. Tree damage also occurred at this location.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado had a peculiar movement from the northeast to the southwest. It initially touched down, and destroyed a 100 foot long pole barn, lofting some of the 1000 pound girders into a nearby field to the west.  The tornado then curled back to the southeast hitting another farmstead further south, collapsing a pole barn and knocking down trees all around a farmhouse. Occupants of the house were home, but were uninjured.

A tornado with a maximum damage rating of EF-2 occurred on this Tuesday evening. Around 3 PM CST, a thunderstorm began forming over Ellis county. This storm rapidly strengthened into a supercell and it split as it moved into Rooks and Osborne counties around 430 PM CST. The slow-moving left-mover produced hail primarily the size of quarters and smaller over Rooks county, but there was a single 3-inch hailstone reported. This storm rapidly weakened by 6 PM CST. The right-mover advanced across Osborne and northern Mitchell counties. This storm produced a tornado that tracked from south central Osborne county to southwest Mitchell county. While this storm was in progress over southern Osborne county, another supercell developed near the town of Osborne. This storm moved northeast across Jewell county from 430 to 545 PM CST, producing hail up to the size of half dollars and possibly a funnel cloud that was reported by a spotter.||Slow-moving 997 mb low pressure was over central Kansas. This low was moving east along a quasi-stationary front that extended from Pennsylvania, across northern Kansas , to Colorado. In the upper-levels, strong southwest flow was over the Central Plains. During the day, a low moved from Utah and Colorado into the Nebraska Panhandle. A subtropical high was over the Gulf of Mexico. There was strong temperature contrast across the front. Temperatures were in the 50s over Nebraska, and in the lower 80s over most of Kansas. Dewpoints over north central Kansas were in the middle 60s to near 70. Mid-level lapse rates were steep (7.5 C/km) resulting in MLCAPE of 2000-3500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 70-80 kt. The greatest low-level shear and SRH were north of the front where low-level winds were significantly backed. Along and south of the front, 0-3 km SRH was around 150 m2/s2, with 0-1 km SRH around 100 m2/s2. The Significant Tornado Parameter was near 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level system surged northeast across Kansas that brought an equally intense surface cyclone across the state in the afternoon. There was a moisture-rich environment entrenched across Kansas.  This combined with the powerful cyclone crossing the state, numerous thunderstorms, several of which were severe, produced torrential rains that caused major flooding and flash flooding from the evening hours the 20th until late in the afternoon on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue occurred in the 900-1000 block of North Park Blvd.

This combined with the powerful cyclone crossing the state, numerous thunderstorms, several of which were severe, produced torrential rains that caused major flooding and flash flooding from the evening hours the 20th until late in the afternoon on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The levee was breached on the west side of the Neosho River just west of Shaw Road and 160th with water covering the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of severe thunderstorms that produced a grand slam of flash floods, large hail, damaging winds & even a couple tornadoes hit nearly all of the Wichita (ICT) County Warning Area (CWA) throughout the afternoon & night on the 24th. Of the severe thunderstorm modes, floods & flash floods proved to be, by far, the greatest threat to these areas. The flash flooding continued across primarily Southeast Kansas well into the 25th and in parts of South-Central Kansas, the morning of the 26th. River flooding continued the following week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds that were estimated around 43 knots/50 mph were reported across Greenwood County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, weak tornado occurred in Rooks County on this Tuesday afternoon. Areas of stratiform rain moved north and northwest across north central Kansas during the morning and early afternoon. This rain was associated with an anomalously strong low pressure system. At sunrise, a 993 mb low was over the Oklahoma panhandle. During the day, this low moved north-northeast and by 3 PM CST, it was located near Hill City and had deepened to 989 mb. The stratiform rain ended across north central Kansas during the mid-afternoon hours as the occluded front moved through. Isolated showers and thunderstorms then formed in the dry slot, underneath the upper low, where clouds had decreased and insolation resulted in destabilization. These showers and storms initially formed over central Kansas and moved north into north central Kansas. Around 315 PM CST, a new cell formed along the Graham-Rooks county border. It moved across northwest Rooks county and at 350 PM CST, it produced a brief EF-0 tornado. Around the time of the tornado, temperatures had risen into the mid to upper 50s, while dewpoints were in the upper 40s. With mid-level lapse rates around 7 C/km, MLCAPE increased to between 500 and 1000 J/kg. Radar sampling was an issue, but this appeared to be a low-top mini-supercell. Effective shear was around 30 kt. 0-1 km SRH was decreasing as low-level winds veered and became increasingly unidirectional. However, 0-1 km shear was around 25 kt. The evening sounding at Dodge City indicated the height of 500 mb pressure level was 5470 m which was a record low for the date. The 500 mb temperature was -19 C which was near the record lowest for the date.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on photographic evidence, this brief tornado actually touched down. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was draped across portions of Kansas during the day on May 7th, 2019 ahead of an approaching storm system. Widespread showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall developed across the area. These storms inundated areas that were already saturated from recent rainfall resulting in widespread flooding across south central Kansas. ||Significant flash flooding occurred during the evening and overnight of May 7th, 2019 into the early morning hours of May 8th. The most significant flash flooding occurred during the early morning hours of May 8th, when areas southeast of Wichita picked up between seven and ten inches of rainfall in a 24 hour period. Numerous water rescues took place across Mulvane, Augusta, Wellington and Rose Hill. In addition, several miles of the Kansas Turnpike was closed south of Mulvane due to the Slate Creek reaching record levels. Closing this much of the Kansas Turnpike due to flooding hasn't happened in over 30 years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over the road, on the south side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed across portions of central Kansas, during the late afternoon and evening of May 5th, 2019.  The supercells initially produced large hail, but quickly became tornado producing storms, as they moved east southeast.  Five different tornadoes ending touching down across Barton, Rice and Reno counties, as the supercell moved to the southeast.  As the supercell moved into the Wichita metro area, the storm produced copious amounts of small hail, that covered ground to almost a foot deep, on the west side of Wichita.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms broke out across Barton & Lincoln Counties in the evening. Then, as evening progressed, the severe thunderstorms spread across most of the remainder of Central Kansas, then across South-Central Kansas overnight, before finally weakening as they approached Greenwood County early in the morning on the 27th. The severe thunderstorms started as hail-producers, then as they surged across Central & South-Central Kansas, became prolific damaging wind-producers with speeds that reached around 61 knots/70 mph. There were also areas of flash flooding, though not nearly as widespread as had been the case for most of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of supercell thunderstorms developed along a slow moving cold front, during the afternoon and evening of May 6th, 2019 into the early morning hours of May 7th. The supercells produced large hail and some damaging winds. As the storms moved south, they began to stall over southern Kansas and produce some flash flooding.  Significant flooding started during the late night hours of May 7th, 2019 when Chanute picked up 5 to 6 inches of rainfall in only a couple hours. This caused widespread flash flooding across the city where numerous water rescues took place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of severe thunderstorms that produced a grand slam of flash floods, large hail, damaging winds & even a couple tornadoes hit nearly all of the Wichita (ICT) County Warning Area (CWA) throughout the afternoon & night on the 24th. Of the severe thunderstorm modes, floods & flash floods proved to be, by far, the greatest threat to these areas. The flash flooding continued across primarily Southeast Kansas well into the 25th and in parts of South-Central Kansas, the morning of the 26th. River flooding continued the following week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flooding was reported just south of the Broadway/103rd South intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level system surged northeast across Kansas that brought an equally intense surface cyclone across the state in the afternoon. There was a moisture-rich environment entrenched across Kansas.  This combined with the powerful cyclone crossing the state, numerous thunderstorms, several of which were severe, produced torrential rains that caused major flooding and flash flooding from the evening hours the 20th until late in the afternoon on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported flowing over Highway 59 and was 8 inches deep for about one eighth of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms with heavy rains caused further flash flooding in parts of South & Southeast Kansas in the morning on the 26th. Thunderstorms that had occurred the previous evening produced, on average, up to around 3 inches of rain. The renewed thunderstorms that occurred the following morning produced another 1 to 3 inches this morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flash floods spread from ditches across a roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 7-9 p.m. CDT on this Tuesday evening, an intense and outflow-dominant storm cluster dropped southward out of Nebraska into primarily Jewell County of this six-county North Central Kansas area, yielding a handful of severe hail and wind reports in a brief time. A few of the more notable reports included golf ball size hail and a measured 66 MPH gust at Formoso, and half dollar size hail and estimated 65 MPH winds near Webber. To the west of the main storm, the combo of radar signatures and nearby reports from extreme southern Nebraska suggested that hail might have also slipped into far northern Smith and northeastern Phillips counties, but there were no verifying reports. Between 8-9 p.m. CDT the severe weather threat quickly waned within the local area as the most vigorous convection departed off to the east-southeast. Backing up in time, the Jewell County severe storm initiated around 4 p.m. CDT as an isolated, high-precipitation supercell roughly 40 miles north of the state line, gradually acquiring a more linear nature before entering North Central Kansas a few hours later. This was a fairly weakly-forced event in the mid-upper levels, with northern Kansas positioned along the southern periphery of broad westerly flow, and no significant shortwave troughs of note. However, the origins of this early-evening convection could be traced to low-level convergence along a west-east oriented, quasi-stationary surface front draped across south central Nebraska. The mesoscale environment became quite supportive of severe storms on either side of the state line, featuring 2000-3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail, along with estimated 65 MPH winds that occurred earlier .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of supercell thunderstorms developed along a slow moving cold front, during the afternoon and evening of May 6th, 2019 into the early morning hours of May 7th. The supercells produced large hail and some damaging winds. As the storms moved south, they began to stall over southern Kansas and produce some flash flooding.  Significant flooding started during the late night hours of May 7th, 2019 when Chanute picked up 5 to 6 inches of rainfall in only a couple hours. This caused widespread flash flooding across the city where numerous water rescues took place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported the gust.
