EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms producing heavy rainfall moved across southeastern Kansas and into Missouri from May 23rd and into the early morning hours of May 25th. The cumulative rainfall caused areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major street flooding was reported across the city of Pittsburg. At least one water rescue was performed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of strong to severe storms that developed to the west and northwest of the Ozarks affected southeast Kansas into west central Missouri. The first occurred during the early morning hours of the 6th and the second round moved pretty much the same area on the late evening of the 6th into the early morning of the 7th. These storms produced hail up to the size of half dollars and wind gusts to 60 mph. Rainfall accumulations averaged around one and a half to two inches in this region, with isolated accumulations over three inches. This caused areas of flash flooding to occur and some rivers to rise back above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported half-dollar sized hail on Bagdad Hill, about 1 mile west of the Missouri/Kansas state line.

Late in evening on the 24th, severe thunderstorms broke out across Central Kansas. The severe thunderstorms produced a trifecta of hail as large as golf balls, winds that reached around 70 mph, and torrential rains that caused flooding and flash flooding. The most significant flooding and flash flooding occurred in Southwest Barton County where several tributaries overflowed requiring sandbagging in and around Claflin. Flooding continued in most of Barton County until early in the morning on the 27th. The thunderstorms continued well into the 25th by which time flooding became the primary, if not sole, threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various country roads were flooded across Southeast Russell County. The roads typically flood during heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving line of severe thunderstorm wind gusts raced from southwest-to-northeast across parts of this six-county North Central Kansas area on this Sunday evening, mainly between 7-9 p.m. CDT. While strong gusts of 40-55 MPH were fairly common across much of the area, the primary swath of damaging winds in the 60-75 MPH range impacted Rooks, Phillips, Smith and northwestern Osborne counties. A few of the most impressive measured gusts from personal weather stations included: 75 MPH south-southwest of Stockton, 73 MPH north-northeast of Codell and 70 MPH near Damar. In addition, airport sensors at Phillipsburg and Smith Center clocked peak gusts of 62 and 59 MPH, respectively. As for actual wind damage, the majority of ground-truth reports focused in Rooks County, including downed power poles in Stockton and Zurich, and large tree branches knocked down in Plainville. ||Tracing this storm complex back to its roots, it originated in east-central Colorado during the early-to-mid afternoon, before occasionally flaring up to severe levels as it traversed parts of northwest Kansas mid-to-late afternoon. However, the aforementioned, concentrated corridor of damaging winds did not really take shape until shortly before storms entered the local area into Rooks County. In the mid-upper levels, the main instigator was a low-amplitude shortwave trough lifting east-northeast into northern Kansas from the west. At the surface, convection was largely tied to a triple point centered over western portions of the Nebraska-Kansas border, featuring the intersection of a 1000 millibar low, a north-south oriented dryline, and a northeast-southwest oriented cold front. Early evening mesoscale convective parameters in North Central Kansas featured around 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of effective deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 65 MPH wind gust was measured by a mesonet station located 3 miles west-southwest of Speed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms producing heavy rainfall, severe winds, hail and tornadoes moved across Southeastern Kansas and into the Missouri Ozarks from May 20th through May 22th as upper level disturbances interacted with a slow moving but powerful storm system over the Central Plains. Tornadoes occurred with each round, with the strongest and most numerous tornadoes occurring during the late afternoon and evening of the 22nd. The cumulative rainfall caused areas of flash flooding with some flooding lingering across the region. Some locations in southeastern Kansas and southwestern Missouri reported in excess of 8 inches of rainfall over the 72 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that an EF-1 tornado with maximum winds of 105 mph traveled along a 6 mile path before entering Barton County Missouri. The tornado continue for a total of 10 miles before lifting SE of Mindenmines, Missouri. This was the second tornado from a supercell that also produced the Weir tornado.  This tornado touched down south of Pittsburg near the intersection of E 50th and S 220th Streets where it uprooted trees and destroyed an outbuilding.  The tornado continued and additionally damaged several more outbuildings and numerous homes south of the Pittsburg Mall. There were varying degrees of damage as as the storm tracked across E 520th, 530th, and then between 240th and  S 270th streets, where it snapped tree trunks, before moving into Barton County, Missouri.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening of May 28 a strong tornado formed in Douglas County and grew to a wide EF-4 tornado. This is the same storm that caused the storm chasing group to fly off the road just south of Lawrence, Kansas. Once the tornado crossed into Leavenworth County it impacted mainly rural residences, but also impact the city of Linwood. It was here that isolated EF-3 and EF-4 damage was noted. The tornado continued on to the Bonner Springs area before it dissipated just southwest of Kansas Speedway and I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rear flank downdraft winds approaching an estimated 80 mph caused widespread tree damage across the Bonner Springs area. Local storm chaser estimated 80 to 100 mph winds in association with these RFD winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two small clusters of severe thunderstorms rolled from northwest-to-southeast across parts of this six-county North Central Kansas area on this Thursday evening, giving rise to several reports of primarily damaging winds within especially the southwest half of Rooks County, but also in Jewell County. In southern/western Rooks County, winds of at least 70-75 MPH rolled through 9-10 p.m. CDT, causing at least minor wind damage in several towns including: Plainville, Damar, Palco, Zurich and Codell. In Plainville, intense winds inflicted fairly extensive tree damage and toppled all but the bottom section of a county communications tower. Five miles south of Plainville, a semi truck was blown over. At nearly the same time as the Rooks County action, a completely separate cluster of severe storms rumbled through Jewell County, with limited reports including estimated 60-65 MPH winds at Burr Oak and a large tree downed at the courthouse in Mankato. ||Taking a closer look at storm evolution within the area, and starting with Rooks County, this intense supercell cluster initiated nearly 150 miles northwest of the area (in southwest Nebraska) during the mid-afternoon. Emergency management and media confirmed considerable tree damage in Plainville, along with a roof blown off of a former lumberyard and all but the bottom section of a county communications tower toppled to the ground. Three transformers were struck by lightning and power lines were downed. An NWS cooperative observer located 4 miles west-northwest of Plainville reported multiple tree limbs downed and chimney damage to their home. A semi-truck was blown over approximately 5 miles south of Plainville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the morning on the 21st, a line of showers produced 55-70 mph winds across parts of Central Kansas. The high winds hit Ellsworth, Saline and Rice counties. The only damage that was reported was in extreme South-Central Rice County where 4 to 6 inch diameter limbs were broken off in Sterling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust was measured by the Ellsworth Airport AWOS. Ten minutes earlier, at 0556 CST, the Ellsworth Airport AWOS measured a 49-knot/56-mph gust. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moved southeastward across the area producing large hail reports during the evening hours of May 5. A second line of storms moved through during the afternoon hours of May 6 producing numerous significant wind and large hail reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in evening on the 24th, severe thunderstorms broke out across Central Kansas. The severe thunderstorms produced a trifecta of hail as large as golf balls, winds that reached around 70 mph, and torrential rains that caused flooding and flash flooding. The most significant flooding and flash flooding occurred in Southwest Barton County where several tributaries overflowed requiring sandbagging in and around Claflin. Flooding continued in most of Barton County until early in the morning on the 27th. The thunderstorms continued well into the 25th by which time flooding became the primary, if not sole, threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of flooding were received on 203rd Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moved southeastward across the area producing large hail reports during the evening hours of May 5. A second line of storms moved through during the afternoon hours of May 6 producing numerous significant wind and large hail reports.

Early in the morning on December 15, 2019 light snow began falling, and increased intensity through the day. While this event was a pretty run of the mill winter storm, it did produce 6 to 8 inches across much of the area. While the forecast and ultimately the system focused across areas along and south of the Missouri River, there was a narrow band of snow that formed across far northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri, bringing a few extra counties north and east of the Missouri River into the higher amounts of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell through the day, ultimately accumulating six to eight inches through the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of generally sub-severe thunderstorms moved through the region during the early morning hours on May 21. This line did produce a brief tornado in Atchison County (KS), which caused some minor damage. Otherwise, these storms didn't produce any more severe winds or hail, but as they moved east wake low winds produced damage consisting of several tree limbs down as well as power lines and power poles. The storms then went on to produce torrential rains which caused areas of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At HWY 92 and 231st the street was impassible due to a large tree across the road from post-storm wake-low winds. Another tree was down at 155th and Ripley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation supercell t-storm developed during the afternoon of May 28th across Lyon County Kansas and tracked northeast through the evening producing several tornadoes.  The strongest tornado was an EF4 violent tornado that tracked just south of Lawrence Kansas northeast into Leavenworth county Kansas.  No fatalities occurred due to the tornadoes with around 17 injuries reported with most of those associated with vehicles that were chasing the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of rotation and debris lofted aloft.  Subsequent follow up revealed no damage however had eyewitness of small brief tornado in field.  Time based on radar and report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening of May 28 a strong tornado formed in Douglas County and grew to a wide EF-4 tornado. This is the same storm that caused the storm chasing group to fly off the road just south of Lawrence, Kansas. Once the tornado crossed into Leavenworth County it impacted mainly rural residences, but also impact the city of Linwood. It was here that isolated EF-3 and EF-4 damage was noted. The tornado continued on to the Bonner Springs area before it dissipated just southwest of Kansas Speedway and I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado is a continuation of the tornado that formed in Douglas County and crossed the Kansas River into Leavenworth County. As the tornado crossed the Kansas River it became very strong and wide. It produced EF-3 damage to residences along the Kansas River. When the tornado impacted Linwood, Kansas it produced isolated EF-3 and EF-4 damage. Preliminary estimates for damage exceeded 26 million dollars, per the Leavenworth Times ( https://www.leavenworthtimes.com/news/20190531/tornado-damage-exceeds-26-million ). Despite the EF-4 rating and widespread devastation to rural areas near Lawrence and Linwood no serious injuries occurred. This was the strongest tornado to impact the Kansas City metro area since 2003.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a fairly windy Thursday afternoon across North Central Kansas, with several hours of sustained northerly speeds commonly 20-30 MPH and frequent gusts around 40 MPH. However, thanks largely to the development of scattered high-based rain showers and a few weak thunderstorms, which enhanced the ambient wind fields, several places experienced brief gusts of 50+ MPH. This included peak gusts at area airports of 51 MPH in Smith Center and Phillipsburg and 55 MPH near Stockton. In addition, one unofficial personal weather station north of Bellaire clocked a severe-criteria gust of 58 MPH around 2 p.m. CDT. There were no known reports of damage from these winds. It is likely that without the presence of shallow convection, wind gusts in most places would have topped out no higher than 45 MPH. Wind profiles from the surface to the mid-levels were modestly strong and unidirectional, but ambient speeds at the 850 millibar level were no higher than 30-40 knots. In addition, the west-east surface pressure gradient at mid-afternoon across the state of Kansas was not overly-supportive of severe winds, averaging only 7-9 millibars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 MPH wind gusts was measured by a mesonet station located 8 miles north of Bellaire.

A line of generally sub-severe thunderstorms moved through the region during the early morning hours on May 21. This line did produce a brief tornado in Atchison County (KS), which caused some minor damage. Otherwise, these storms didn't produce any more severe winds or hail, but as they moved east wake low winds produced damage consisting of several tree limbs down as well as power lines and power poles. The storms then went on to produce torrential rains which caused areas of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a water rescue near Bonner Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy late winter and spring precipitation, mixed with a loaded snow pack caused the Missouri River to flood in March. Consistent heavy rains continued the Missouri River flooding into and through May. Some locations along the Missouri River were in major flood for several weeks as heavy rains continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy spring rains caused ongoing flooding along the Missouri River to Continue through the month. Some locations along the Missouri River experienced major flooding at times during the month. Damage estimates from roads washed out and crop damage are unknown at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the morning on December 15, 2019 light snow began falling, and increased intensity through the day. While this event was a pretty run of the mill winter storm, it did produce 6 to 8 inches across much of the area. While the forecast and ultimately the system focused across areas along and south of the Missouri River, there was a narrow band of snow that formed across far northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri, bringing a few extra counties north and east of the Missouri River into the higher amounts of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell through the day, ultimately accumulating six to eight inches through the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in evening on the 24th, severe thunderstorms broke out across Central Kansas. The severe thunderstorms produced a trifecta of hail as large as golf balls, winds that reached around 70 mph, and torrential rains that caused flooding and flash flooding. The most significant flooding and flash flooding occurred in Southwest Barton County where several tributaries overflowed requiring sandbagging in and around Claflin. Flooding continued in most of Barton County until early in the morning on the 27th. The thunderstorms continued well into the 25th by which time flooding became the primary, if not sole, threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Dispatch reported that numerous streets were flooded in Great Bend.

A high precipitation supercell t-storm developed during the afternoon of May 28th across Lyon County Kansas and tracked northeast through the evening producing several tornadoes.  The strongest tornado was an EF4 violent tornado that tracked just south of Lawrence Kansas northeast into Leavenworth county Kansas.  No fatalities occurred due to the tornadoes with around 17 injuries reported with most of those associated with vehicles that were chasing the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado appears to have formed just east of Lone Star Lake around 605 pm CDT where it absorbed a smaller circulation and associated tornado as it intensified and move northeast across Douglas county including areas just south and southeast of the city of Lawrence.  The tornado became wide and intense as it moved south of Lawrence and crossed highway 10 before moving just north of Eudora and over the Kansas river into Leavenworth county.  The damage  path was one half to one mile at times with the most intense damage surveyed in the high end EF3 range which suggests winds of 150 to 160 mph.   The tornado ended in Leavenworth county around 7 pm CDT with a path of around 28 miles total.  The tornado did produce EF4 damage in Leavenworth county around Linwood Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Monday marked the first true very windy day of the fall season across North Central Kansas, as the early morning and daytime hours featured sustained northwesterly winds commonly 25-35 MPH, frequent gusts of 40-55 MPH and a few unofficial High Wind Warning criteria gusts of 58+ MPH (all severe gusts occurred within Rooks County). The first few warning-level gusts actually happened well before sunrise, as mesonet stations in Plainville and north of Codell gusted from 58-62 MPH in association with the initial surge of wind wrapping around the southwest side of a 998 millibar surface low centered over the eastern NE-SD border. Then during the daylight hours, the combination of deepening diurnal mixing and a further intensification of the parent low pressure system to around 986 millibars (over southern MN) instigated the main round of intense northwest winds, with additional gusts of 58-65 MPH clocked at mesonets near Stockton and Codell. At its strongest, the west-to-east surface pressure gradient across the state of Kansas reached a respectable 15 millibars, ranging from 998 in the far northeast to 1013 far southwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 62 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located 8 miles north-northeast of Codell.

During a roughly 14-hour period from the early evening of Wednesday the 10th into the morning of Thursday the 11th, this six-county portion of North Central Kansas observed a fairly classic clash of the seasons (spring versus winter). While parts of the area saw a varied mix of sleet, brief freezing rain and up to 1 of snow as colder air rushed in (most accumulating snow was in Phillips/Smith counties), wintry weather was not really a big deal for the local area. Instead, the main story involved a few strong to severe thunderstorms. Two storms in particular featured ground-truth of severe criteria hail. The first tracked through eastern Mitchell County mainly 7:00-7:30 p.m. CDT, with quarter to golf ball size hail reported in the Asherville area. Then, a few hours later (10:00 -10:30 p.m. CDT), another fast-paced hail storm raced northward across western Phillips County, dropping quarter size stones on Prairie View. All local convection occurred on the colder side of a surface front that had already surged southward into central Kansas by early evening. In fact, the Phillips County severe storm occurred with surface temperatures only in the 30s! Despite these chilly readings, the mesoscale environment was primed for hail storms, with elevated most-unstable CAPE up to around 1000 J/kg and stout effective deep-layer wind shear of 50-70 knots. Regarding the bigger-picture meteorological setup, the driving force in the mid-upper levels was a large-scale trough over the Central Plains, anchored by a closed low pressure system that slowly migrated from eastern Colorado on the evening of the 10th to far northeast Nebraska by early-evening on the 11th. During this same 24-hour time frame, the associated strong surface low pressure center started out over south central Kansas on the evening of the 10th (roughly 986 millibars). By early-evening on the 11th, it had drifted to the NE/SD/IA border area, and weakened slightly to around 992 millibars.

Then during the daylight hours, the combination of deepening diurnal mixing and a further intensification of the parent low pressure system to around 986 millibars (over southern MN) instigated the main round of intense northwest winds, with additional gusts of 58-65 MPH clocked at mesonets near Stockton and Codell. At its strongest, the west-to-east surface pressure gradient across the state of Kansas reached a respectable 15 millibars, ranging from 998 in the far northeast to 1013 far southwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located 8 miles north-northeast of Codell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation supercell t-storm developed during the afternoon of May 28th across Lyon County Kansas and tracked northeast through the evening producing several tornadoes.  The strongest tornado was an EF4 violent tornado that tracked just south of Lawrence Kansas northeast into Leavenworth county Kansas.  No fatalities occurred due to the tornadoes with around 17 injuries reported with most of those associated with vehicles that were chasing the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employees surveyed the track and found damage beginning around 2 miles southeast of Overbrook that continued northeast into Douglas county.  The sporadic damage path continued to just east southeast of Lone Star Lake in Douglas County where it merged with a larger circulation that eventually become the long track violent tornado. The Osage county portion only had EF0 damage associated with it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado occurred just to the northeast of the city of Overbrook around 8 pm on the evening of September 27th.  Large tree limbs were snapped and did damage to the roof of a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small brief tornado hit a home doing damage to large tree limbs which fell onto the home's roof.  A large satellite dish that was bolted to a concrete pad was also tossed into a cornfield across the road to the northeast.   Estimated winds around 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving line of severe thunderstorm wind gusts raced from southwest-to-northeast across parts of this six-county North Central Kansas area on this Sunday evening, mainly between 7-9 p.m. CDT. While strong gusts of 40-55 MPH were fairly common across much of the area, the primary swath of damaging winds in the 60-75 MPH range impacted Rooks, Phillips, Smith and northwestern Osborne counties. A few of the most impressive measured gusts from personal weather stations included: 75 MPH south-southwest of Stockton, 73 MPH north-northeast of Codell and 70 MPH near Damar. In addition, airport sensors at Phillipsburg and Smith Center clocked peak gusts of 62 and 59 MPH, respectively. As for actual wind damage, the majority of ground-truth reports focused in Rooks County, including downed power poles in Stockton and Zurich, and large tree branches knocked down in Plainville. ||Tracing this storm complex back to its roots, it originated in east-central Colorado during the early-to-mid afternoon, before occasionally flaring up to severe levels as it traversed parts of northwest Kansas mid-to-late afternoon. However, the aforementioned, concentrated corridor of damaging winds did not really take shape until shortly before storms entered the local area into Rooks County. In the mid-upper levels, the main instigator was a low-amplitude shortwave trough lifting east-northeast into northern Kansas from the west. At the surface, convection was largely tied to a triple point centered over western portions of the Nebraska-Kansas border, featuring the intersection of a 1000 millibar low, a north-south oriented dryline, and a northeast-southwest oriented cold front. Early evening mesoscale convective parameters in North Central Kansas featured around 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of effective deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 63 MPH wind gust was measured by a mesonet station located 8 miles north of Bellaire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms moved into southeast Kansas during the early morning of the 22nd and spread across the rest of the region through the afternoon leading to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer reported 2.78 inches of rain in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms affected mainly areas north of Interstate 44 from the morning of the 28th to the morning of the 29th. Wind damage occurred in Crawford, KS county with the latter round of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts damaged nine outbuildings on the south side of Mulberry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe t-storms moved across parts of north central and northeast Kansas during the early morning hours of May 24th.  Two small tornadoes did occur and produced damaged to parts of the city of Marysville.  The storm produced another small tornado in Nemaha county.  Both were rated EF1 based on damage indicators provided by EM and other sources of imagery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos submitted show damage along a nearly 10 mile long track from just southwest of Marysville through parts of the city then into rural Marshall county.  Damage indicators and degree of damage suggest EF1 intensity with winds to around 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely powerful Severe thunderstorms that produced a trifecta of large (in a few instances very large) hail, damaging winds that reached as high as 80 mph, and flash floods struck parts of Central as well as most of South-Central and Southeast Kansas in the afternoon and evening of the 22nd. Hardest hit were Reno and Sedgwick counties where hail reached the size of baseballs in Reno County and widespread winds from 70 to 80 mph whipped across most of Sedgwick County, most notably around the Wichita Metro area where a 67-mph gust was measured at Eisenhower Airport. No doubt, tree damage was widespread, among which were large trees that were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon and early evening of the 21st, three short-lived tornadoes struck Southeast Kansas. The first and third events were very brief, but the second caused minor damage in Elk County 2 miles west of Longton where it caused roof damage to a home and shifted an outbuilding from its foundation. Later that evening, an isolated severe thunderstorm lashed Russell County with very high winds where a 65-knot gust was measured at Russell Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was some roof damage to a home and an outbuilding was shifted from its foundation. There was also some damage to chicken coops. The time of the event was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm briefly become strong enough to produce nickel size hail over southern Jewell county on this Saturday evening. During the early evening hours, an area of weak thunderstorms had been occurring over central Kansas. Around 630 PM CST, a few isolated thunderstorms began developing to their north, over Osborne, Jewell, and Mitchell counties. The small storm that moved east along the Jewell-Mitchell county line became strong enough, for a short time, to produce hail up to the size of nickels 6 miles south of the town of Jewell.||This storm formed along a weak front that had been moving north through Kansas during the day, but had slowed its northward progress by 6 PM CST. Late in the afternoon, a weak 1008 mb low had formed along this front over north central Kansas. In the upper-levels, the Westerlies were fairly weak, zonal, and confined to the northern half of the U.S. A weak shortwave trough was over the Dakota's. This storm formed in a zone of warm air advection. Around the time of convective initiation, temperatures were in the lower 80s, with dewpoints in the middle 70s. The mid-level lapse rate was poor, but the MLCAPE was still around 3000 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 35-40 kt. The lack of forcing aloft, and only weak warm air advection, are probably what precluded a greater coverage and severity of storms.

Low-end severe hail fell in a few locations on this Monday evening. Around 5 PM CST, isolated thunderstorms began developed along a line from Hayes, KS to Hebron, NE. Numerous other thunderstorms soon filled in across north central Kansas, from Natoma to Osborne to Mankato. Between 630 and 7 PM CST, other isolated cells began developing northwest of this line. One of these isolated storms formed over eastern Rooks county. This storm moved north-northeast into Smith county where it produced hail up to the size of half dollars in Athol at 745 PM CST. Other storms produced dime to penny size hail in Natoma, and lots of small hail up to the size of pennies in Burr Oak. By 730 PM CST, a squall line had evolved across Jewell, Mitchell and eastern Osborne counties. This line exited east and south of north central Kansas by 9 PM CST with no other strong or severe storms being reported.||These storms developed along a slow-moving cold front. At the upper-levels, a weak shortwave trough was moving through in moderately strong southwest flow. A longwave trough was over the West, with a subtropical high over Georgia. Just prior to the storm initiation, temperatures were in the middle 80s, with the dewpoints in the middle to upper 60s. With a poor mid-level lapse rate, MLCAPE was only 1000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was about 30 kt.

Storms developed ahead of a cold front and upper level trough during the late afternoon of the 12th across southeast Kansas. The heaviest rainfall occurred over southeast Kansas near Girard where over three inches of rain led to flash flooding on state Highways 47 and 3 west of Girard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowing over Highway 47 near Highway 3 closed the road to traffic in several places west of Girard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail when it hit Barnard which is located far Northeast Lincoln County. The hail must've lasted a few minutes because the spotter reported that it rapidly covered his yard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter reported a copious amount of 1-inch hail that rapidly covered his yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely powerful Severe thunderstorms that produced a trifecta of large (in a few instances very large) hail, damaging winds that reached as high as 80 mph, and flash floods struck parts of Central as well as most of South-Central and Southeast Kansas in the afternoon and evening of the 22nd. Hardest hit were Reno and Sedgwick counties where hail reached the size of baseballs in Reno County and widespread winds from 70 to 80 mph whipped across most of Sedgwick County, most notably around the Wichita Metro area where a 67-mph gust was measured at Eisenhower Airport. No doubt, tree damage was widespread, among which were large trees that were uprooted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from tennis ball to baseball sized. No damage had been reported yet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moving northeastward from southern California on the 27th to the Central Plains on the 29th deepened a surface low and brought warm and moist Gulf air into the region. Strong to severe storms with heavy rainfall developed over the region during the afternoon and evening of the 28th. The strongest storms occurred along a squall line that moved from southeast Kansas into western and central Missouri during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy snow fell across the north central sections of Kansas.  Snow totals were quick to pile up and varied from 2 to 9 inches across the area.  Further to the south, lighter amounts of snow fell along with patches of freezing rain.  This weather definitely impacted travel with one fatal accident near Wichita.  Temperatures in the vicinity of the accident were in the upper 20s to near 30 degrees through the morning. Light snow was mixed with sleet and freezing drizzle from late morning into the early afternoon. Slick roadways were reported throughout the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report via Facebook of a snow depth of 6 inches was received at the NWS from Lindsborg, KS.  Several other reports of snow amounts ranging from 1.5 to 4 inches were noted across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred behind a deep, occluded low on this Saturday. At daybreak, a 987 mb low was near central Nebraska with an occluded front extending southeastward to the triple point near Springfield, MO. Through the day, this low slowly migrated across northern Nebraska to near Sioux City, IA by sunset. The tight pressure gradient in the western semi-circle of the low gradually enveloped the region resulting in strengthening winds. Winds were strongest between noon and midnight CST. During that time frame, northwest winds were sustained at 25 to 35 MPH, with frequent gusts between 45 and 50 MPH. At times, winds were sustained around 40 MPH. Less frequent peak gusts were mostly between 50 and 60 MPH. The highest measured wind gusts were 62 MPH at Hunter, and 58 MPH near Codell, Bellaire, and Speed. As is typical for this part of the nation, there was very little in the way of damage or power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding area observations, sustained speeds of at least 40 MPH and isolated gusts close to 65 MPH likely occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light freezing rain event on December 27th, combined with some gusty winds on December 28th, led to power outages and road closures for parts of northwest Kansas on the evening of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported that there were numerous power outages in Cheyenne County, including in Bird City around 7 PM local time on the evening of the 28th. Light freezing rain had been observed the previous day on the 27th with the Goodland, KS ASOS (KGLD) recording 0.15 inches of ice accumulation, with an additional 0.05 inches of ice measured by the KGLD ASOS on the evening of the 28th. Winds on the 28th then gusted to about 40 mph which was enough to cause power outages as ice-covered power lines succumbed to the gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early Sunday morning on December 15th.  The snow was characterized by smaller bands of moderate to heavy snow which repeatedly moved across the area between 6 am and 6 pm on Sunday.  The banded nature of the snow resulted in localized areas of heavy snow in excess of 6 inches while other spots received 2 to 5 inches.  The heaviest snow fell along and north of I-70 and mainly north and west of Topeka.  Lighter amounts fell south and east of Topeka.  In addition there was a brief period of light freezing drizzle or light freezing rain that caused a minor glaze mainly south of Interstate 70 as the event was ending late in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of spotters and cocorahs reports showed a swatch o 6-8 inches of snow fell across Dickinson, into Riley, Geary, Pottawatomie, Wabaunsee and Jackson Counties.  There were some localized reports of 8-10 inch amounts but most measurements were in the 6 to 8 inch range.  All of this snow fell between 6 am and 6 pm on Sunday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Up to an estimated six inches of snow fell over a small part of extreme northwest corner of Phillips county on this Tuesday. Most of the northern and western parts of the county were covered by 3 to 4 inches, with less elsewhere. Only 0.6 was measured at Phillipsburg. Around 5 AM CST, a shield of snow began moving into north central Kansas from the west. This initial burst of snow moved out of Phillips county by 730 AM CST, and out of north central Kansas by 9 AM CST. The area of precipitation in the deformation zone was still organizing during the morning, but by early afternoon, a well-defined shield of snow extended from eastern Colorado across northwest Kansas into south central Nebraska. North central Kansas was on the southeastern fringe of this snowy system. Steady light to occasionally moderate snow fell across northern and western portions of Phillips county from 1 PM CST until 530 PM CST. During the evening, one last band of light snow traversed Phillips, Smith, and Jewell counties between 7 and 1030 PM CST, but with little additional accumulation as winds were gusting between 30 and 35 kt.  Phillips and Smith county schools cancelled classes.||At 6 AM CST, a stationary front extended from the Great Lakes to Oklahoma to 988 mb low pressure near Pueblo, Colorado. Through the day, this low moved east-northeast across Kansas, arriving at Kansas City by 9 PM CST and filling to 993 mb. In the upper-levels, a longwave trough was over the western United States. A strong shortwave trough was moving through it, over the Desert Southwest. As this trof moved over the Plains, a low formed over Kansas. By 6 PM CST, 500 mb winds had strengthened to 120 kt at Oklahoma City. By dawn the next day, the low was over Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts across the county ranged anywhere from less than 1 inch to 6 inches, with the highest reports coming from the far northwest corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the evening of Saturday the 14th and the morning of Sunday the 15th, nearly this entire six-county North Central Kansas area saw up to a 12-hour period of light snow. This was a fairly low-impact event, as amounts in most spots only averaged 1-2, especially within the northern local counties of Phillips, Smith and Jewell. In addition, winds were notably light, generally 5-10 MPH throughout. However, the southern row of counties (Rooks/Osborne/Mitchell) received a more widespread coverage of 2.5 to 4. Per several CoCoRaHS and official NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest totals featured: 4 in Covert and nine miles east of Hunter, 3.8 at Natoma and 3 in Beloit.||Timing-wise, the first flakes flew on the evening of the 14th, but the majority of snow accumulated between 3-11 a.m. on the 15th while gradually ending from west-to-east. In the mid-upper levels, forcing was keyed by the passage of a few low-amplitude waves embedded within strong quasi-zonal flow. At the surface, the light winds stemmed from the local area residing within the southern/western periphery of an expansive high (central pressure around 1022 millibars) sliding southeastward from the eastern Dakotas into Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow totals included 3 at Beloit and 2.5 at Cawker City (NWS coop observers) and 4 nine miles east of Hunter (CoCoRaHS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system lifted northeast across South-Central Kansas throughout the day and evening and combined with a surface front in southeast Kansas to produce widespread moderate across most of Central Kansas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few hours after midnight snow began to move into Northwest Kansas from the west.  Wind gusts of 30-40 MPH occurred with the snow.  The band of heavy snow from Colorado moved into Northwest Kansas during the late morning, gradually weakening in the early afternoon.  Near blizzard conditions occurred under the band of snow as it proceeded eastward.  Total snowfall amounts ranged from almost 10 inches near the Cheyenne/Rawlins County line to an inch or so south of I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 9.5 inches at Bird City to around 7 inches near the Cheyenne/Sherman County line.  Highway 36 was closed from St. Francis west during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Kansas lost 5 people to vehicle accidents on November 22nd where ice and snow played a role.  There was 1 fatality in the NWS Topeka service area in Dickinson County.  Generally the area received 1-3 inches of snow with some light freezing drizzle and sleet also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 19 year old male was killed in a multiple vehicle crash where ice and snow played a role. There were 2 other injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely powerful Severe thunderstorms that produced a trifecta of large (in a few instances very large) hail, damaging winds that reached as high as 80 mph, and flash floods struck parts of Central as well as most of South-Central and Southeast Kansas in the afternoon and evening of the 22nd. Hardest hit were Reno and Sedgwick counties where hail reached the size of baseballs in Reno County and widespread winds from 70 to 80 mph whipped across most of Sedgwick County, most notably around the Wichita Metro area where a 67-mph gust was measured at Eisenhower Airport. No doubt, tree damage was widespread, among which were large trees that were uprooted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The winds were estimated as being greater than 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire which started in Cheyenne County, KS during elevated fire weather conditions quickly spread throughout the northeast portions of the county, requiring the response of local, state, and federal agencies to fight the fire. Local emergency management found several ignition points along and east of KS Hwy 27 in northwest Kansas. Prevailing winds from the southwest thankfully prevented the fire from spreading into the nearby towns of St. Francis and Wheeler, KS. Initial response from local city and county fire departments had to be quickly supplemented with support from the Kansas Department of Emergency Management, the Kansas National Guard, and the National Weather Service. By the time the fire was fully extinguished on November 10th, around six thousand acres of mainly grassland had been burned by the Cherry Creek fire according to estimates from local emergency management. A state disaster declaration was made by Kansas Governor Laura Kelly in order to aid response operations. No significant structures are known to have burned as a result of this wildfire, however some damage was done to a small cemetery near Wheeler when grasses burned through that area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The start time of the wildfire complex, around 1130 AM CST, was based on radar observations of smoke plumes which were present for several hours on Saturday, November 9th. Over 31 fire starts occurred along Highways 27 and 36, consuming an estimated total of 6,000 acres. Local, state, and federal agencies were involved in the response to the fire through the following day, Sunday, November 10th. NWS Goodland provided on-site support starting the morning of November 10th to protect the property and personnel for the ongoing response.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Arctic cold front pushed through the area with strong mixing behind it.  The result was wind gusts frequently to 50 MPH with occasional gusts approaching 60 MPH.

A strong shortwave trough pushed through the area out of northeastern New Mexico and the panhandles of Oklahoma and Texas.  With southwest flow aloft and moist southeast flow near the surface, a large area of precipitation developed and quickly changed to snow. Locations closer to Interstate 70 reported widespread 5 to 9 inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 6 inches was common across the county, especially closer to Interstate 70 where most amounts were 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely powerful Severe thunderstorms that produced a trifecta of large (in a few instances very large) hail, damaging winds that reached as high as 80 mph, and flash floods struck parts of Central as well as most of South-Central and Southeast Kansas in the afternoon and evening of the 22nd. Hardest hit were Reno and Sedgwick counties where hail reached the size of baseballs in Reno County and widespread winds from 70 to 80 mph whipped across most of Sedgwick County, most notably around the Wichita Metro area where a 67-mph gust was measured at Eisenhower Airport. No doubt, tree damage was widespread, among which were large trees that were uprooted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst caused major damage along Tank Ave. between 4th and 8th streets. Trees were uprooted and power poles were snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled and stationary front was the focus for thunderstorm developed during the late afternoon and evening.
A s/wv trough initiated thunderstorms across the Oklahoma and Texas panhandles and this activity spread northeast across the area.
QLCS tornadoes occurred along a squall line as it swept across the region.

Given that several official NWS cooperative observer stations within this six-county North Central Kansas area realized their wettest month of August on record (including Plainville 4WNW, Natoma and Beloit), it should come as no surprise that there were at least a few rounds of flooding rains. For some areas mainly south of the Highway 36 corridor, the roughly 48-hour period from the morning of Friday the 23rd through the pre-dawn hours of Sunday the 25th was one of the wettest stretches of the month, with widespread rain totals commonly 2-5+. Over the course of these few days, the main event focused during a 12-hour block between 5 p.m. CDT on the 24th and 5 a.m. CDT on the 25th, as numerous, slow-moving thunderstorms traversed the area. Although radar signatures suggested there might have been a few instances of large hail/damaging winds late in the evening (as storms organized into a linear MCS before departing southward into central Kansas), there was no ground-truth verification. Certainly though, the main story of the night was heavy rain and flooding, especially within Rooks, northern Osborne and far southern Smith counties, where a few mesonets confirmed totals of 4-5 in the Downs, Portis and Harlan areas. Limited ground-truth flash flooding reports included a gravel road washed out north of Webster State Park and several inches of water flowing over a gravel road southeast of Alton. Undoubtedly, several rural roads and small creeks experienced short-term flooding from this heavy rain event. In addition, several area river gauges breached at least minor flood stage for anywhere from a few hours to a few days, including: Solomon River near Glen Elder, North Fork Solomon near Portis and South Fork Solomon near Woodston. More notably, the South Fork Solomon near Osborne went into moderate flood, with a crest of 21.37 on the evening of the 25th preliminarily ranking as the 5th-highest on record (and  highest since 1993).||In the mid-upper levels, the Central Plains was mired in a multi-day stretch of somewhat active and wet weather during this time frame. Specifically from the 24th into the 25th, a low-amplitude disturbance tracked southeastward from the Central High Plains toward central Kansas, downstream from a larger-scale trough centered over southwestern Canada. As typical, the maintenance/intensification of convection after dark was augmented by warm air/moisture advection within a modest, 25-35 knot low level jet axis. Heavy rain rates were supported by a moist profile featuring precipitable water values of 1.50-2.00.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding washed out County Road 6, just south of County Road H.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe storms developed over southeast Kansas on June 1st, 2019. Hail up to quarter size was reported with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gentleman was ejected from an ATV when he encountered a washed out section of road from heavy rains 5 to 6 days earlier.  He landed in a puddle of water and the ATV then landed on top of him pinning him to the ground and under water causing him to drown.||The road in question did not have any flowing water at the time and was dry with a few residual puddles remaining. ||Rainfall from several days earlier caused minor field flooding in which water ran from one field across the road into another field. The flow of water eroded portions of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in evening on the 24th, severe thunderstorms broke out across Central Kansas. The severe thunderstorms produced a trifecta of hail as large as golf balls, winds that reached around 70 mph, and torrential rains that caused flooding and flash flooding. The most significant flooding and flash flooding occurred in Southwest Barton County where several tributaries overflowed requiring sandbagging in and around Claflin. Flooding continued in most of Barton County until early in the morning on the 27th. The thunderstorms continued well into the 25th by which time flooding became the primary, if not sole, threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads across roughly the western half of the county were flooded. Sections of West Barton County Road were closed. Sandbagging occurred in and around Pawnee Rock due to swollen tributaries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moved southeastward across the area producing large hail reports during the evening hours of May 5. A second line of storms moved through during the afternoon hours of May 6 producing numerous significant wind and large hail reports.

Several strong to (a few) severe thunderstorms rumbled across much of this six-county North Central Kansas area on this early Saturday morning, mainly between midnight and 4 a.m. CDT. Ground-truth verification of hail and strong winds was quite limited, with a few of the only reports consisting of nickel size hail in southern Mitchell County and a mesonet-measured 73 MPH gust in eastern Rooks County. However, winds this strong appeared to be quite isolated and there were no known damage reports. Rainfall-wise, the vast majority of the area measured no more than 0.50-1.25, although a CoCoRaHS observer in Beloit received 1.97. ||For the first few hours of this event (midnight-2 a.m. CDT) storms were fairly isolated in nature, gradually developing within the exit region of a 30-40 knot southwesterly low level jet. Over the following few hours convection increased in coverage, briefly forming a nearly solid line within Rooks/Osborne/Mitchell/Jewell counties before weakening and dissipating. In the mid-upper levels, broad quasi-zonal flow was in place over the Central Plains. The overnight mesoscale environment over North Central Kansas was certainly supportive of severe storms, featuring a decent combination of 2000-3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of deep-layer wind shear. At the surface, dewpoints averaged in the low-70s F as the local area resided north of a somewhat loosely-defined, quasi-stationary front stretched through central KS.

Very few locations were spared from the severe storms that moved across the area at various times the 17th and 18th.  Winds up to 70 mph and hail to golf ball size were noted along with locations of very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trains derailed near Walton.  Ninety rail cars were derailed on one train and fifty cars were derailed on the other.  Many of the cars were double stacked. All cars were empty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening of the 21st, severe thunderstorms hit parts of South-Central Kansas. The primary severe modes were 60-70 mph winds and torrential rains that caused flash flooding. Perhaps hardest hit was Sedgwick County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at the Oliver/Lincoln Intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in evening on the 24th, severe thunderstorms broke out across Central Kansas. The severe thunderstorms produced a trifecta of hail as large as golf balls, winds that reached around 70 mph, and torrential rains that caused flooding and flash flooding. The most significant flooding and flash flooding occurred in Southwest Barton County where several tributaries overflowed requiring sandbagging in and around Claflin. Flooding continued in most of Barton County until early in the morning on the 27th. The thunderstorms continued well into the 25th by which time flooding became the primary, if not sole, threat.

Severe thunderstorms lashed much of east and southeast Kansas with 60-70 mph winds as they crossed these areas. By far, hardest hit was Montgomery County where the 60-70 mph winds occurred. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms developed across the area, especially along and southeast of the Kansas Turnpike. In addition to large hail and damaging winds, widespread flash flooding occurred in east-central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was running across Highway 58.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonably-typical, mainly marginally-severe mesoscale convective system (MCS) tracked from west-to-east across North Central Kansas on this early Sunday morning, primarily affecting areas south of the Highway 36 corridor with strong winds and heavy rainfall. There were only a few ground-truth reports of severe-criteria gusts, from mesonet stations near Stockton (59 MPH), Cawker City (75 MPH, along with a report of tree limbs down at Glen Elder State Park), and Beloit (73 MPH). Rainfall-wise, the heaviest overnight totals of 2.00-3.50 soaked southern portions of Rooks and Osborne counties, including a CoCoRaHS total of 3.50 in Plainville. ||Long before reaching North Central Kansas, the origins of this MCS began as scattered storms developing over southeast Colorado during the late afternoon of the 10th. As the evening wore on, this convection gradually congealed into a more organized complex as it initially drifted northeast into northwestern Kansas before taking a more easterly turn after dark, eventually reaching Phillips/Rooks counties between 12:30-1:30 a.m. CDT and exiting the local area out of Jewell/Mitchell counties by 4 a.m. CDT. In the wake of the main storm complex, widespread showers/non-severe storms lingered over parts of the area until shortly after sunrise. In the mid-upper levels, fairly weak zonal flow was in place over the Central Plains, to the north of a large-scale ridge centered over TX. Over North Central Kansas, the overnight mesoscale environment featured healthy instability (evidenced by surface dewpoints into the 70s F and mixed-layer CAPE 2000-3000 J/kg), but only 20-30 knots of deep-layer wind shear. However, the eastward propagation of the MCS was aided by warm air/moisture advection within the exit region of a 30 knot, southerly low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 MPH wind gust was measured by a mesonet station located 1 mile south-southwest of Stockton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell t-storms developed quickly along an arc from north central east southeast through northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and early evening on Tuesday May 21st. The supercells occurred in an environment conducive to rapid low level rotation and tornado formation.  At least 6 tornadoes were documented between 4pm and 9 pm during that evening.  Some were longer track tornadoes with the strongest being rated an EF3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This short-lived but powerful tornado tracked through northern Nemaha County and damaged several structures. The most severe damage occurred to a home towards the middle of the tornado's path, where low end EF-3 damage was noted. Damage to trees and power lines also occurred. The tornado lifted about 200 yards south of the Nebraska state line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms developed across the area, especially along and southeast of the Kansas Turnpike. In addition to large hail and damaging winds, widespread flash flooding occurred in east-central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 59 has water across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe t-storms moved across north central Kansas and did produce one small weak tornado in Washington County around 920 pm CDT Sunday May 26th. An outbuilding was destroyed and windows were broken on another school building in Linn, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One outbuilding destroyed. Powerlines were down. 0.5 miles east, an old abandoned house was damaged with large trees snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms lashed much of east and southeast Kansas with 60-70 mph winds as they crossed these areas. By far, hardest hit was Montgomery County where the 60-70 mph winds occurred. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated around 56 knots/65 mph pulled a 50 foot by 30 foot pole barn from the ground and lifted it. The pole barn landed in a nearby pasture. Posts had been inserted 3 feet deep but did not have concrete support. There was an 8-foot opening to the north and a 15-foot opening to the west. A small lawn tractor that had been parked on the east side of the building was pushed onto its right side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms lashed much of east and southeast Kansas with 60-70 mph winds as they crossed these areas. By far, hardest hit was Montgomery County where the 60-70 mph winds occurred. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A delayed report was received from an employee with The Marion County Record of wind damage across Marion County. The worst damage occurred across the northern half of Marion County; most of it to trees and power lines. Some of the trees were large but there were no specifics as to their diameters. Most of the town of Marion was reported with no power after the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm Chasers reported brief touchdown in open field.  This tornado then moved into Wabaunsee County through open pasture.

Minor tree and tree limb damage was reported with this tornado however location suggests this could have been associated with RFD on south side of circulation and not tornado itself.  Therefore it will be rated EFU.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms producing heavy rainfall, severe winds, hail and tornadoes moved across Southeastern Kansas and into the Missouri Ozarks from May 20th through May 22th as upper level disturbances interacted with a slow moving but powerful storm system over the Central Plains. Tornadoes occurred with each round, with the strongest and most numerous tornadoes occurring during the late afternoon and evening of the 22nd. The cumulative rainfall caused areas of flash flooding with some flooding lingering across the region. Some locations in southeastern Kansas and southwestern Missouri reported in excess of 8 inches of rainfall over the 72 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that an EF-1 tornado with maximum winds of 100 mph touched down just west of the Missouri state line and traveled a half mile to the northeast before passing into Missouri. As it crossed the border the storm intensified to an EF3 with an estimated 140 mph winds. This tornado was produced by the supercell that spawned the Treece tornado and continued to produce funnel clouds very near the ground through Riverton and the north Galena area before the tornado touched down near Woodchuck Drive and caused roof damage to two homes and downed several trees as it crossed into Missouri along SE 118th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved across portions of southeast Kansas during the afternoon hours.  One individual was struck by lightning in Montgomery county and passed away.  Elsewhere, heavy rains caused street flooding in Iola.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in Iola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled out frontal system near the Colorado, Kansas and Nebraska border area resulted in scattered thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours. The storms generally moved southeast, producing severe wind gusts, large hail and a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement and public reports of a tornado. Viewed from Sherman County, looking south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a roughly 14-hour period from the early evening of Wednesday the 10th into the morning of Thursday the 11th, this six-county portion of North Central Kansas observed a fairly classic clash of the seasons (spring versus winter). While parts of the area saw a varied mix of sleet, brief freezing rain and up to 1 of snow as colder air rushed in (most accumulating snow was in Phillips/Smith counties), wintry weather was not really a big deal for the local area. Instead, the main story involved a few strong to severe thunderstorms. Two storms in particular featured ground-truth of severe criteria hail. The first tracked through eastern Mitchell County mainly 7:00-7:30 p.m. CDT, with quarter to golf ball size hail reported in the Asherville area. Then, a few hours later (10:00 -10:30 p.m. CDT), another fast-paced hail storm raced northward across western Phillips County, dropping quarter size stones on Prairie View. All local convection occurred on the colder side of a surface front that had already surged southward into central Kansas by early evening. In fact, the Phillips County severe storm occurred with surface temperatures only in the 30s! Despite these chilly readings, the mesoscale environment was primed for hail storms, with elevated most-unstable CAPE up to around 1000 J/kg and stout effective deep-layer wind shear of 50-70 knots. Regarding the bigger-picture meteorological setup, the driving force in the mid-upper levels was a large-scale trough over the Central Plains, anchored by a closed low pressure system that slowly migrated from eastern Colorado on the evening of the 10th to far northeast Nebraska by early-evening on the 11th. During this same 24-hour time frame, the associated strong surface low pressure center started out over south central Kansas on the evening of the 10th (roughly 986 millibars). By early-evening on the 11th, it had drifted to the NE/SD/IA border area, and weakened slightly to around 992 millibars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to golf ball size hail was reported in the Asherville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving line of severe thunderstorm wind gusts raced from southwest-to-northeast across parts of this six-county North Central Kansas area on this Sunday evening, mainly between 7-9 p.m. CDT. While strong gusts of 40-55 MPH were fairly common across much of the area, the primary swath of damaging winds in the 60-75 MPH range impacted Rooks, Phillips, Smith and northwestern Osborne counties. A few of the most impressive measured gusts from personal weather stations included: 75 MPH south-southwest of Stockton, 73 MPH north-northeast of Codell and 70 MPH near Damar. In addition, airport sensors at Phillipsburg and Smith Center clocked peak gusts of 62 and 59 MPH, respectively. As for actual wind damage, the majority of ground-truth reports focused in Rooks County, including downed power poles in Stockton and Zurich, and large tree branches knocked down in Plainville. ||Tracing this storm complex back to its roots, it originated in east-central Colorado during the early-to-mid afternoon, before occasionally flaring up to severe levels as it traversed parts of northwest Kansas mid-to-late afternoon. However, the aforementioned, concentrated corridor of damaging winds did not really take shape until shortly before storms entered the local area into Rooks County. In the mid-upper levels, the main instigator was a low-amplitude shortwave trough lifting east-northeast into northern Kansas from the west. At the surface, convection was largely tied to a triple point centered over western portions of the Nebraska-Kansas border, featuring the intersection of a 1000 millibar low, a north-south oriented dryline, and a northeast-southwest oriented cold front. Early evening mesoscale convective parameters in North Central Kansas featured around 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of effective deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds as high as 75 MPH raced across Rooks County. Measured wind gusts included 75 MPH at 7:37 PM CDT by a mesonet site located 5 miles south-southwest of Stockton, 73 MPH at 7:44 PM CDT by a mesonet site located 8 miles north-northeast of Codell, and 70 MPH at 7:37 PM CDT by a mesonet site located 1 mile south-southwest of Damar. Power poles were knocked down in both Stockton and Zurich, and tree limbs of 6 to 8 inches in diameter were downed in Plainville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms that produced large hail and damaging winds moved across the southern sections of the state.  Two very weak landspouts were also reported.  Also, very heavy rains with many locations across the southern half of Kansas reporting rainfall amounts well in excess of 2 inches over the previous 24 hours.  The highest amounts greater than 3 inches were listed individually.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very weak landspout from a non-supercell storm touched down and lingered for 4 minutes over open country.
